paddington.history: One of the original seven Metropolitan Railway stations, Paddington opened on 10 January 1863 to connect passengers at Brunel's Great Western Railway terminus with the rest of the city. The sub-surface platforms - used by Circle, District, and Hammersmith & City trains - were built by cut-and-cover and still feel recognisably Victorian. The Bakerloo line arrived in 1913, adding the station's first deep-tube connection. The opening of the Elizabeth line in 2022 was the most significant expansion in over a century: new platforms 24 metres below ground running east to Tottenham Court Road in under 10 minutes and west to Heathrow in 30.
funFacts: Paddington was one of the original seven stations when the Metropolitan Railway opened on 10 January 1863, built to connect passengers arriving at Brunel's Great Western Railway terminus with the rest of London.

The sub-surface platforms were built using cut-and-cover construction - essentially a giant trench dug from street level, roofed over once the tracks were laid. The broad, shallow tunnels that resulted still define the character of the station today.

Paddington's Elizabeth line station, opened in 2022, sits 24 metres below ground and features a vaulted concrete ceiling spanning the full width of the box - an engineering feat on a scale the station had not seen since 1863.

paddington-elizabeth.history: One of the original seven Metropolitan Railway stations, Paddington opened on 10 January 1863 to connect passengers at Brunel's Great Western Railway terminus with the rest of the city. The sub-surface platforms - used by Circle, District, and Hammersmith & City trains - were built by cut-and-cover and still feel recognisably Victorian. The Bakerloo line arrived in 1913, adding the station's first deep-tube connection. The opening of the Elizabeth line in 2022 was the most significant expansion in over a century: new platforms 24 metres below ground running east to Tottenham Court Road in under 10 minutes and west to Heathrow in 30.
funFacts: Paddington was one of the original seven stations when the Metropolitan Railway opened on 10 January 1863, built to connect passengers arriving at Brunel's Great Western Railway terminus with the rest of London.

The sub-surface platforms were built using cut-and-cover construction - essentially a giant trench dug from street level, roofed over once the tracks were laid. The broad, shallow tunnels that resulted still define the character of the station today.

Paddington's Elizabeth line station, opened in 2022, sits 24 metres below ground and features a vaulted concrete ceiling spanning the full width of the box - an engineering feat on a scale the station had not seen since 1863.

baker-street.history: Baker Street was one of the original seven stations on the Metropolitan Railway when it opened on 10 January 1863 - the first day of service on the world's first underground railway. Built by cut-and-cover beneath Marylebone Road, the sub-surface platforms are among the oldest continuously used underground platforms in the world. New lines arrived steadily: the Bakerloo in 1906, the Jubilee in 1979. Baker Street is unusual on the Underground in having platforms for five different lines across multiple levels. Its proximity to 221B Baker Street - the fictional address of Sherlock Holmes - brought literary tourists long before the Holmes silhouette tiles were added in a 1980s refurbishment.
funFacts: The station opened alongside the world's first underground railway but looks nothing like its 1863 self: successive rebuilds for the Bakerloo (1906), Jubilee line (1979), and various expansions have layered 160 years of Underground architecture into a single complex.

The station is decorated with Sherlock Holmes silhouette tiles throughout, referencing the fictional detective whose address at 221B Baker Street is a short walk away - a detail added during a 1980s refurbishment.

Baker Street serves five different lines across multiple levels: the sub-surface Metropolitan, Circle, and Hammersmith & City lines share a broad Victorian station above, while the Bakerloo and Jubilee lines run through separate deep-level tunnels below.

kings-cross.history: The Metropolitan Railway first served King's Cross in 1863, though the station has grown far beyond those origins. Deep-level Piccadilly and Northern line stations followed in 1906 and 1907, designed by Leslie Green in his ox-blood terracotta style. On 18 November 1987 a fire swept through the station, ignited by a match dropped onto grease-laden escalator treads; 31 people died and the disaster led directly to a permanent ban on smoking across the entire Underground. A £1 billion modernisation completed in 2012 created a unified ticket hall beneath King's Cross and St. Pancras International. Today it is the busiest interchange on the Underground, serving six lines.
funFacts: King's Cross St. Pancras serves six lines - Victoria, Piccadilly, Northern, Metropolitan, Circle, and Hammersmith & City - more than any other station on the network.

On 18 November 1987 a fire at King's Cross killed 31 people, caused by a match dropped onto grease-laden wooden escalator treads. The disaster led to a permanent ban on smoking throughout the entire Underground system.

The connection to St. Pancras International makes this station a gateway to mainland Europe: Eurostar services reach Paris in just over two hours and Brussels in under two, all from the same ticket hall complex.

edgware-road.history: Edgware Road opened as one of the original seven stations on the Metropolitan Railway on 10 January 1863 from Paddington to Farringdon - the world's first underground railway journey. It is one of the few central London Underground stations to run at street level rather than underground, with trains passing through an open cutting. The station serves the Metropolitan, Hammersmith & City, and Circle lines. A separate District line station of the same name stands a short distance to the south, resulting in two unconnected tube stations with identical names on the same street.

edgware-road-district.history: The District line's Edgware Road station opened as part of the Inner Circle construction in the 1870s. It stands a short distance south of the Metropolitan Railway's station of the same name - a geographical quirk that results in two entirely unconnected tube stations with identical names on the same street. The District line platforms are below ground level, in contrast to the open-cutting Metropolitan line station nearby. Passengers transferring between the two must exit and walk between them.

great-portland-street.history: Great Portland Street opened in 1863 as Portland Road, one of the original seven stations on the Metropolitan Railway. It was renamed Great Portland Street in 1917. The station serves the southern edge of Regent's Park and the medical district around Harley Street - widely regarded as the world's most concentrated area of private specialist medical practices. University College London and the cluster of teaching hospitals around Euston Road are within walking distance to the east.

euston-square.history: Euston Square opened in 1863 as Gower Street, one of the original Metropolitan Railway stations. It was renamed Euston Square in 1909, though it sits some distance from Euston mainline station. The station is surrounded by university buildings - University College London and several University of London colleges are immediately adjacent - as well as the Wellcome Collection and a cluster of hospitals. It is among the busiest stations on the Circle line for student traffic during term time.

farringdon.history: Farringdon - originally Farringdon Street - was the eastern terminus of the Metropolitan Railway on 10 January 1863, one of the two endpoints of the world's first underground railway journey. It sat at the foot of the Snow Hill tunnel, which from 1866 allowed through trains between the Metropolitan and the London, Chatham and Dover Railway south of the Thames. That link fell dormant for most of the 20th century until Thameslink restored it in 1988. The Elizabeth line's arrival in 2022 completed the transformation: Farringdon now sits at the intersection of two of London's most important cross-city routes.
funFacts: Farringdon - originally called Farringdon Street - was the eastern terminus of the Metropolitan Railway on 10 January 1863, one of the two endpoints of the world's first underground railway journey.

The station is now served by four lines (Metropolitan, Circle, Hammersmith & City, and Elizabeth), more than at any point in its 160-year history, with the Elizabeth line alone doubling the number of passengers passing through each day.

Smithfield Market, one of the largest wholesale meat markets in the world and active near this site since the 10th century, sits directly above the northern entrance - deliveries to its traders were once made via a dedicated underground siding from the station.

hammersmith.imageUrl: Hammersmith tube station (Circle and Hammersmith & City lines)
hammersmith.history: Hammersmith (Metropolitan line) opened in 1864 as the western terminus of a joint Metropolitan and Great Western Railway branch from Paddington. The station has been rebuilt several times and the current structure dates largely from the 1990s. It serves as the western terminus of the Hammersmith & City and Circle lines. A separate District and Piccadilly line Hammersmith station stands approximately 300 metres to the east, occupying a physically distinct site that has been a separate station since 1874.

hammersmith-district.imageUrl: Hammersmith tube station (District and Piccadilly lines)
hammersmith-district.history: Opened on 9 September 1874 as the western terminus of the District Railway on the Broadway side of Hammersmith, physically about 300 metres from the Metropolitan Railway's station on the Grove Road side. The District Railway had been pushing westward from Westminster and Hammersmith became its outermost terminus before further extensions were built. The Piccadilly line began serving the station on 15 December 1906, running as a western branch off the main Piccadilly route. The station offers cross-platform interchange between District and Piccadilly trains, one of the most convenient interchanges on the western Underground.

shepherd-bush.history: Opened on 30 July 1900 as one of the original stations on the Central London Railway - the electric forerunner of today's Central line. The line was immediately dubbed 'the Twopenny Tube' for its flat two-penny fare regardless of distance, a radical pricing model that made it one of the most heavily used railways in London within months of opening. A Metropolitan Railway station of the same name operated nearby from 1864; it was renamed Shepherd's Bush Market in 1914 to avoid confusion and today serves the Hammersmith & City line.
funFacts: The Central London Railway's flat-fare experiment was so successful that other tube companies were forced to reduce their own fares - the Twopenny Tube essentially set the pricing expectations for the entire underground network.

latimer-road.history: Opened in 1868 on the Hammersmith & City Railway. One of the quieter stations on the line, it was built to serve the rapidly expanding residential district of North Kensington.

royal-oak.history: Opened in 1871, Royal Oak is one of the smallest stations on the network. It stands in a cutting just west of Paddington and was added after the original line opened to serve the surrounding neighbourhood.

westbourne-park.history: Opened in 1866 on the Metropolitan Railway, the station is built in an open cutting and retains much of its Victorian character. It stands close to the Grand Union Canal.

notting-hill-gate.history: Notting Hill Gate opened on 1 October 1868 on the Metropolitan District Railway. The area takes its name from a historic turnpike gate, long demolished. The station was substantially rebuilt in 1959 when the District and Circle line sub-surface platforms were integrated with the existing Central line deep-level tube. The Notting Hill Carnival, Europe's largest street festival, passes within metres of the station every August Bank Holiday - the station typically handles some of its highest annual passenger volumes during carnival weekend.

high-street-kensington.history: High Street Kensington opened on 1 October 1868 on the Metropolitan District Railway's extension from South Kensington toward Paddington. The station serves one of London's main retail and residential high streets, sitting between Kensington Palace to the north and the Royal Borough of Chelsea to the south. The sub-surface cut-and-cover station retains much of its Victorian structure beneath a busy shopping street.

gloucester-road.history: Gloucester Road opened on 24 December 1868 originally called Brompton (Gloucester Road) by the Metropolitan District Railway. The station is notable for its disused eastbound platform, which has been converted into a publicly visible exhibition space for emerging artists - one of a small number of active Underground stations where passengers can view art displayed in a former platform from a moving train.
funFacts: The unused platform at Gloucester Road has been an exhibition space since 2000. The works are visible to passengers on passing Circle and District trains, making Gloucester Road one of the few places on the Underground where art is integrated into the operational railway environment rather than displayed separately in ticket halls or corridors.

south-kensington.history: South Kensington opened on 24 December 1868 at the junction of the Metropolitan and District Railways, whose commercial rivalry meant each company operated its own separate platform section here for decades. The station is the gateway to one of the most remarkable concentrations of museums anywhere in the world: the Natural History Museum, Victoria and Albert Museum, and Science Museum are all within a short walk, connected to the station by a network of subway passages extended in the 1880s specifically to serve the growing cultural quarter. On school holidays and summer weekends the station handles some of the highest visitor volumes on the Circle and District lines.
funFacts: The subway tunnels beneath Exhibition Road connecting the station to the museums were built in the 1880s as part of the Circle line cut-and-cover infrastructure - originally intended as the start of a westward Underground extension. When the extension was abandoned, the tunnels were repurposed as permanent pedestrian passages to the museum complex, a role they still serve today.

The Natural History Museum, V&A, and Science Museum were all built on land purchased from the profits of the Great Exhibition of 1851 - an area Prince Albert promoted as 'Albertopolis'. South Kensington station was designed as the gateway to this cultural district from the beginning.

aldersgate.history: Opened on 23 December 1865 as Aldersgate Street on the Metropolitan Railway's extension toward Moorgate. The station was renamed several times - shortened to Aldersgate in 1910, becoming Aldersgate and Barbican in 1923, and finally simplified to Barbican in 1968. It serves the Barbican Centre, Europe's largest multi-arts complex, which opened in 1982 on a site largely destroyed by wartime bombing. The Barbican estate surrounding the station houses around 4,000 residents and was Grade II listed in 2001 as an important example of post-war urban planning.
funFacts: The Barbican estate was built on land devastated by Blitz bombing in 1940 - one of the most heavily bombed areas in the City of London. Construction took nearly 40 years from the end of the war; the final residential blocks were completed in 1976. The estate's concrete brutalist architecture was controversial for decades before being widely reassessed as a significant work of 20th-century design.

moorgate.history: Moorgate opened on 23 December 1865 as 'Moorgate Street', extending the Metropolitan Railway into the City of London for the first time. In its Victorian heyday it had four tracks handling heavy peak flows of City workers, and became an interchange with the City & South London Railway in 1900 and later with the Great Northern & City Railway, whose tunnels were built to mainline loading gauge for intercity services that never fully materialised. On 28 February 1975 a southbound Northern City Line train failed to stop and hit the end wall at full speed, killing 43 people - the worst peacetime accident in Underground history. The cause was never established.
funFacts: On 28 February 1975 a Northern City Line train failed to stop and struck the end wall of the tunnel at full speed, killing 43 people - the worst peacetime accident in London Underground history. The driver's actions were never explained.

Moorgate opened in December 1865 as 'Moorgate Street' on the Metropolitan Railway, extending the line into the City of London for the first time and triggering rapid commercial development in the surrounding streets.

The Northern City Line platforms at Moorgate - now used by Thameslink trains to Hertfordshire - were built to full mainline loading gauge, part of a Victorian plan to run intercity trains directly into the Underground that was never completed.

ladbroke-grove.history: Opened in 1864 as Notting Hill, the station was renamed several times - becoming Ladbroke Grove in 1919. It serves the northern end of the Portobello Road area, one of London's most well-known street markets and antiques districts. The station was part of the original Hammersmith and City Railway and remains on the Hammersmith & City and Circle lines.

bayswater.history: Bayswater opened on 1 October 1868 on the Metropolitan District Railway's extension from South Kensington toward Paddington. It was renamed Bayswater (Queen's Road) & Westbourne Grove in 1923, shortened to Bayswater (Queen's Road) in 1933, and reverted to plain Bayswater in 1946. The station sits beneath Queensway, serving a densely populated neighbourhood that has historically attracted a cosmopolitan mix of residents and a high concentration of hotels and short-term accommodation. The area immediately north of the station borders Hyde Park, one of the largest urban parks in Europe.
funFacts: About 200 metres east of the station, on the elegant residential terrace of Leinster Gardens, stand two of London's most discreet pieces of railway infrastructure: numbers 23 and 24 are not houses at all but elaborate false stucco facades, built in 1868 to disguise the gap above the Metropolitan Railway's cut-and-cover tunnel where the line briefly surfaces between Bayswater and Paddington to vent its steam locomotives. The fakes preserve the unbroken Bayswater terrace from the eyesore of an open railway cutting, and the only signs that anything is amiss are the windows, painted onto the brickwork, and the absence of letterboxes or doorbells.

sloane-square.history: Sloane Square opened on 24 December 1868 as one of the original District Railway stations. Its construction required dealing with the River Westbourne, one of London's buried 'lost rivers', which could not be diverted and was instead enclosed in a large iron pipe that passes directly through the station above the tracks - still visible today, crossing the platform near the westbound end. The Westbourne feeds the Serpentine in Hyde Park before continuing south to the Thames at Chelsea. The station was badly damaged by a German bomb on 12 November 1940, which killed 37 people sheltering inside; the current structure largely dates from the post-war reconstruction.
funFacts: The iron pipe carrying the Westbourne through the station is one of the most photographed features on the Underground. The Westbourne is one of several 'lost rivers' buried as London expanded, and Sloane Square is one of the very few places in the city where one of these hidden watercourses is directly visible above ground level.

victoria.history: Victoria opened on 24 December 1868 on the District Railway, built to serve the London, Brighton and South Coast Railway's terminus nearby. Circle line services followed in 1878. The Victoria line - which takes its name from this station - arrived in December 1968, exactly a century after the District Railway first opened the sub-surface platforms, and brought a deep-level connection that transformed Victoria into one of the network's key north-south interchanges. The mainline terminus above serves Gatwick Airport and the south coast and is among the busiest in Britain, generating a constant flow of passengers between rail and Underground throughout the day.
funFacts: Victoria mainline station was the London departure point for the revived Orient Express from 1982 until 2009, when the luxury train's London services ended. Passengers bound for Venice or Istanbul checked in at a dedicated terminal within the station.
The Victoria line opened at this station on 7 December 1968, completing the southern section of the line from Walthamstow - but Brixton, the current southern terminus, didn't open until July 1971.

st-james-park.history: St James's Park opened on 24 December 1868 by the District Railway, one of the original stations along the Thames Embankment. The station sits directly beneath 55 Broadway - the headquarters of what is now Transport for London, designed by Charles Holden in 1929. The building's sculptural programme included works by Eric Gill and Jacob Epstein, two of which caused a public controversy on their unveiling in 1929 and required intervention from the building's owners.
funFacts: The Jacob Epstein reliefs on 55 Broadway above the station - depicting Day and Night - caused one of the 20th century's first major public art scandals when unveiled in 1929. Press and public objected to their explicit style; one relief was modified following the outcry. The episode established a pattern for controversy around Epstein's public commissions that persisted throughout his career.

westminster.history: Westminster opened on 24 December 1868 as Westminster Bridge, one of the original District Railway stations along the Thames Embankment. The 1999 Jubilee line extension required excavating to 39 metres below street level directly beneath Parliament Square, in heavily saturated ground with the Thames just metres away. The resulting Jubilee line concourse - designed by architects Michael Hopkins & Partners - exposes its raw concrete and steel structure to create a deliberately industrial underground space quite unlike anywhere else on the network. Westminster now serves Parliament, the Supreme Court, the Cabinet Office, and Westminster Abbey, and is one of the most heavily used stations on the Jubilee line.
funFacts: The excavation for the Jubilee line platforms had to avoid destabilising the foundations of the Houses of Parliament and the Clock Tower (Big Ben) directly above. Engineers used ground-freezing techniques to stabilise the saturated ground and drilled through layers of London clay monitored centimetre by centimetre to protect the Victorian foundations overhead.

A pedestrian tunnel once ran directly from Westminster station to the Palace of Westminster, allowing MPs and peers to travel to the Underground without surfacing. The tunnel was closed and sealed after a security review following the March 2017 attack on Westminster Bridge.

embankment.history: Embankment opened on 30 May 1870 as 'Charing Cross' on the District Railway, built within Joseph Bazalgette's newly completed Victoria Embankment - one of Victorian London's most ambitious engineering projects, which reclaimed a wide strip of the tidal Thames to carry the railway, a new sewer, gas pipes, and a broad road simultaneously. The station has been renamed several times, becoming Embankment in 1976 to reduce confusion with Charing Cross mainline station. It serves as an interchange between the District, Circle, Bakerloo, and Northern lines via connecting passages, and sits directly beneath Hungerford Bridge with the river just metres to the south.
funFacts: The station's platforms stand on land that was tidal Thames riverbed before the 1860s - Bazalgette's Embankment reclaimed it from the river, and the District Railway was laid on this new ground within a few years of the reclamation being completed.

temple.history: Temple opened on 30 May 1870 on the District Railway within the Victoria Embankment. The station serves the Inns of Court - the Middle Temple and Inner Temple are directly adjacent - and the legal and publishing district of the Strand. Temple is one of a small number of Underground stations that closes entirely on Sundays, reflecting the low demand when the law courts and legal offices are not operating. The station's platforms sit on the Embankment, with the Thames visible from the footbridge above.

blackfriars.history: Blackfriars opened on 30 May 1870 by the District Railway within the Victoria Embankment. The station takes its name from the medieval Dominican priory of Blackfriars, dissolved under Henry VIII in 1538, whose remains lie beneath the streets to the north. The Blackfriars Railway Bridge adjacent to the station was converted into a platform for Thameslink services in 2012, extending across the river - the only railway station in the world with platforms on a bridge over the Thames.

mansion-house.history: Mansion House opened on 3 July 1871 as the eastern terminus of the District Railway, one station short of the originally planned Tower Hill terminus. Prime Minister William Gladstone attended the opening banquet. The station remained a terminus until the completion of the Inner Circle in 1884 connected it onward to Cannon Street. It takes its name from the Mansion House across the road - the official residence of the Lord Mayor of the City of London since 1752.

liverpool-street.history: Liverpool Street tube station opened on 12 July 1875 as part of the Metropolitan Railway's eastward extension from Moorgate, a year after the mainline terminus opened nearby. The Central line arrived in 1912, and the station gradually became one of the most heavily used interchanges in the City of London. Liverpool Street mainline handles services to East Anglia, Stansted Airport, and Hertfordshire, generating a consistent flow between rail and Underground. The Elizabeth line's arrival in 2022 significantly increased the station's scale and importance, connecting it directly west to the West End and Heathrow and east to Canary Wharf.
funFacts: Between 1938 and 1940, Liverpool Street mainline station was the arrival point for nearly 10,000 Jewish children brought to Britain from Nazi-occupied Europe on the Kindertransport. A memorial at the station, unveiled in 2006, commemorates them.

aldgate.history: Opened on 18 November 1876 as the eastern terminus of the Metropolitan Railway, extending the line from Bishopsgate (now Liverpool Street) into the historic East End for the first time. Aldgate takes its name from one of the seven original gates of Roman Londinium, demolished in 1761. For eight years it operated as a dead-end terminus until the Inner Circle was completed on 6 October 1884, when the station became a through stop on what is now the Circle and Hammersmith & City lines. The surrounding area has been a point of arrival for successive waves of immigration into London for centuries - from Huguenot refugees in the 17th century to Jewish communities in the 19th and Bangladeshi communities in the 20th.
funFacts: Aldgate was one of the final links needed to close the Inner Circle - for eight years from 1876 it sat as a Metropolitan Railway dead-end, requiring passengers heading east or south to change to other transport. The completion of the link to Mark Lane (now Tower Hill) in 1884 finally joined the circle.

tower-hill.history: Tower Hill completed the Inner Circle on 6 October 1884, opening as Mark Lane - named after a nearby street in the City. A temporary Metropolitan Railway station called Tower of London had served the same site since 1882 while the permanent building was under construction. The station was renamed Tower Hill in 1946. In 1967 a rebuilt station opened slightly further east on the same alignment, with the Victorian structure demolished; the replacement serves today's Circle and District lines. The Tower of London was still used as a military barracks at the time the first temporary station opened here in 1882.
funFacts: A section of the original Roman city wall of Londinium - built in the 2nd century AD - runs directly alongside the station entrance on Tower Hill. It is one of the best-preserved stretches of the Roman wall in London and is visible from the street outside, making Tower Hill one of the few tube stations where Roman-era archaeology is immediately visible from the exits.

monument.history: Monument opened on 6 October 1884 as Eastcheap on the Metropolitan and District Railway, renamed Monument within weeks. It forms a combined station with Bank, connected by a long underground passage, making the Bank-Monument complex one of the largest interchange points on the network. The station takes its name from the Monument to the Great Fire of London - the 62-metre stone column designed by Christopher Wren stands a short walk from the entrance.
funFacts: The Monument is exactly 62 metres tall and stands exactly 62 metres from the site of the bakery on Pudding Lane where the Great Fire of London began in 1666. Wren designed this precisely so that the column could theoretically function as a giant measuring instrument - if it were tipped over, it would point directly to the fire's origin.

cannon-street.history: Cannon Street opened on 6 October 1884 as one of the final stations completing the Inner Circle on the Metropolitan and District Railway. It sits directly beneath Cannon Street mainline terminus, built by the South Eastern Railway in 1866. The station name is a corruption of 'Candlewick Street', which ran through this part of the City for centuries. Nearby excavations have revealed remains of the Roman Governor's palace, which occupied a large area of this part of Londinium.

finchley-road.history: Opened on 30 June 1879 by the Metropolitan Railway as part of its extension northward from Swiss Cottage toward Willesden Green and eventually Harrow, Finchley Road sits in a deep cutting on the slopes below Hampstead. The station was transformed when the Bakerloo line took over the Stanmore branch on 20 November 1939, building its own deep-level platforms beneath the existing Met cutting and closing three intermediate Metropolitan stations between here and Baker Street within months: Lord's (originally St John's Wood Road), Marlborough Road, and the Metropolitan's own Swiss Cottage. Metropolitan trains have since run non-stop on the original 1868 alignment between Baker Street and Finchley Road, while the parallel deep tubes (Bakerloo until 1979, Jubilee thereafter) stop at the newly-built Swiss Cottage and St John's Wood as their intermediate calls.
funFacts: Lord's station carried that name for only 161 days. Opened in 1868 as St John's Wood Road, it was renamed Lord's on 11 June 1939 to capitalise on the cricket-ground association nearby - only to close on 19 November the same year when the Bakerloo line took over the Stanmore branch and rendered the Metropolitan's intermediate stations redundant. Its sealed platforms and access stairs survive within the Met tunnels and are occasionally visible from passing trains.

Finchley Road has one of the most efficient interchange layouts on the Underground: Metropolitan and Jubilee trains share island platforms, so passengers transfer simply by walking across. The arrangement was built into the 1939 Bakerloo extension specifically to make the cross-line interchange seamless, and remains one of only a handful of stations on the network where switching lines requires no stairs or corridors.

swiss-cottage.history: Opened on 13 April 1868 by the Metropolitan Railway as the temporary northern terminus of its branch from Baker Street, Swiss Cottage held that role for eleven years until the line was extended onward to West Hampstead and Finchley Road in 1879. The name predates the railway: Ye Olde Swiss Cottage, a chalet-styled pub built at the road junction above in 1804, was the inspiration. The Metropolitan station closed on 18 August 1940 - rendered redundant when the Bakerloo line took over the Stanmore branch the previous November and opened its own deep-level Swiss Cottage a few yards away on 20 November 1939, the two stations briefly operating in parallel through the early months of the Blitz. The deep-tube station, designed by Stanley Heaps, passed from the Bakerloo to the Jubilee on 1 May 1979.
funFacts: The area's name predates the railway by more than half a century. Ye Olde Swiss Cottage, the mock-Swiss-chalet pub at the road junction immediately above the station, was built in 1804 when alpine style was fashionable in English landscape design. The pub still stands, much rebuilt, and the station name has commemorated it through three different railway companies and four different lines.

For nine months between November 1939 and August 1940 two separate Swiss Cottage stations operated simultaneously, a few yards apart - the original 1868 Metropolitan station in its sub-surface cutting, and the new Bakerloo deep-tube station that had just taken over services to Stanmore. The Met station finally closed in August 1940 as part of the rationalisation that ended the Metropolitan's intermediate calls between Baker Street and Finchley Road.

st-johns-wood.history: The current St John's Wood station opened on 20 November 1939 as part of the Bakerloo line's takeover of the Stanmore branch from the Metropolitan Railway. It replaced an older Met station a short distance south - originally opened on 13 April 1868 as St John's Wood Road, briefly renamed Lord's in June 1939 to capitalise on the cricket-ground association, and closed in November the same year when the new deep-tube station opened. Designed by Stanley Heaps, London Transport's chief architect for the Bakerloo extension, the new station features a circular ticket hall with art-deco bronze uplighters, a flat concrete ceiling and curved built-in benches - widely considered one of the finest examples of Heaps's interwar Underground design, and Grade II listed for its architectural significance. The station passed from the Bakerloo to the Jubilee on 1 May 1979.
funFacts: St John's Wood is the nearest Underground station to 3 Abbey Road - the EMI recording studio where The Beatles recorded most of their albums between 1962 and 1969, and famously walked across the zebra crossing outside for the cover of Abbey Road in 1969. On warm weekends the station still handles a steady flow of tourists making the short walk to the crossing.

The Metropolitan Railway's rename of the old St John's Wood Road station to 'Lord's' was timed for the start of the 1939 cricket season - a final commercial play just months before the Bakerloo's new deep-tube station rendered it redundant. The name 'Lord's' lasted only 161 days before closure on 19 November 1939, making it one of the shortest-lived station names in Underground history.

harrow-on-the-hill.history: Opened on 2 August 1880 as the temporary northern terminus of the Metropolitan Railway's extension from Baker Street. It became a major junction station as the Met continued to expand northward into the Chilterns. The line reached Pinner in 1885, Rickmansworth in 1887 and Aylesbury by 1892, but it was the opening of the Uxbridge branch from Harrow on 4 July 1904 that established the station's permanent role as the Met's principal suburban junction. Harrow's transformation into the heart of 'Metro-land' followed: the Metropolitan Railway coined the term in 1915 as a marketing brand for the residential suburbs strung along its line, and Harrow Hill - with the spire of St Mary's twelfth-century parish church and the buildings of Harrow School (founded 1572) silhouetted above the station - became the campaign's single most reproduced visual motif. Through its development arm Metropolitan Railway Country Estates Ltd, formed in 1919, the Met itself laid out garden suburbs at Pinner, Rayners Lane, Harrow Garden Village and Northwood Hills, with Harrow-on-the-Hill serving as their gateway station. It remains the busiest stop on the Metropolitan line outside central London, and is shared with Chiltern Railways' main-line services to Aylesbury and Birmingham.
funFacts: The word 'Metro-land' was coined in 1915 by James Garland of the Metropolitan Railway's publicity department, originally for an annual guidebook promoting the residential suburbs along the Met. The campaign ran until the Met was absorbed into the London Passenger Transport Board in 1933, and across nearly twenty years of brochures, posters and timetables Harrow Hill - with the spire of St Mary's and the buildings of Harrow School cresting it - was the single most frequently reproduced image, effectively the brand's mascot.

The Metropolitan Railway was the only Underground company permitted to develop surplus land for housing, a legacy of its mid-Victorian railway acts that the District, Central, Piccadilly and other tube operators never shared. Metropolitan Railway Country Estates Ltd, formed in 1919, used that quirk to lay out whole garden suburbs - Harrow Garden Village, Pinner, Rayners Lane, Northwood Hills - making the Met simultaneously a transport operator and a major suburban property developer, a combination unique on the British rail network.

holland-park.history: Opened on 30 July 1900 on the Central London Railway. Named after Holland House, a nearby Jacobean mansion, the station serves one of London's most attractive residential neighbourhoods.

queensway.history: Opened on 30 July 1900 as 'Queen's Road' on the Central London Railway, renamed Queensway in 1946. The station serves the busy Queensway shopping street and is close to Kensington Gardens.

lancaster-gate.history: Opened on 30 July 1900 on the Central London Railway. Named after the nearby Lancaster Gate entrance to Hyde Park, the station serves the north side of Hyde Park and Bayswater.

marble-arch.history: Marble Arch opened on 30 July 1900 on the Central London Railway. The arch was designed by John Nash as a ceremonial entrance to Buckingham Palace and relocated to its current position at the northeast corner of Hyde Park in 1851 when the palace was enlarged. The surrounding area was the site of the Tyburn gallows - London's principal place of public execution from medieval times until 1783, where an estimated 50,000 people were hanged over four centuries.
funFacts: A small memorial tablet on the traffic island at the base of the arch marks the traditional site of the Tyburn gallows. The station exit brings passengers to within metres of what was once London's busiest execution ground - the site is now one of the most heavily trafficked road junctions in central London.

bond-street.history: Bond Street opened on 30 July 1900 on the Central London Railway, serving one of London's most prestigious shopping streets. New Bond Street and Old Bond Street together form a continuous luxury retail strip that has been the address of choice for jewellers, art dealers, and couturiers since the 18th century. The station became a major interchange with the opening of the Jubilee line in 1979 and was further expanded as part of the Elizabeth line works completed in 2022.

oxford-circus.history: Oxford Circus opened on 30 July 1900 as one of the original Central London Railway stations, serving what was already one of London's busiest retail junctions. The Bakerloo line arrived in 1906 and the Victoria line in 1969, making it a three-line interchange and consistently one of the highest-footfall stations on the network. The three deep-level tube stations were connected over time by a series of underground passages, including a set of diagonal subways running beneath the road junction above - allowing passengers to transfer between all three lines without surfacing. Oxford Street handles an estimated 200 million shoppers a year.
funFacts: During the busiest pre-Christmas Saturdays, Oxford Circus regularly hits its passenger capacity before midday and restricts entry for much of the afternoon - one of only a handful of stations on the network where this happens routinely outside of events.

tottenham-court-road.history: Opened on 30 July 1900 on the Central London Railway, Tottenham Court Road sits at one of the West End's busiest junctions. In 1984 the artist Eduardo Paolozzi created a celebrated series of large mosaic works lining the station's corridors - bold, figurative pieces drawing on mythology and science that became among the best-known public art commissions on the Underground. The station was almost entirely rebuilt for the Elizabeth line: a new ticket hall opened under Dean Street and the Elizabeth line platforms opened in May 2022. During the reconstruction, parts of Paolozzi's mosaic work were carefully dismantled and some panels relocated, though a significant portion was lost in the process.
funFacts: Paolozzi's mosaics, installed 1984-1986, covered around 950 square metres of wall and tunnel space - one of the largest underground public art commissions in Britain at the time. Paolozzi was a leading figure of British Pop Art and a co-founder of the Independent Group, whose ideas about mass imagery and mythology ran through the entire design.

Tottenham Court Road station sits at the edge of what was once London's densest concentration of hi-fi, electronics, and computer shops - a character Tottenham Court Road had from the 1960s until the 2010s, and which was at its commercial peak during exactly the years Paolozzi's mosaics were installed.

holborn.history: Holborn opened on 30 July 1900 as British Museum on the Central London Railway, renamed Holborn in 1933 when the Piccadilly line platforms opened. The original British Museum station on the Central line closed the same day - its tunnel and platform still exist nearby, used for equipment testing. The Piccadilly line's Aldwych branch diverges from the main line just west of Holborn; Holborn itself is the nearest station to the British Museum and to Lincoln's Inn Fields, one of London's largest public squares.
funFacts: The original British Museum station, bricked up since 1933, has been the subject of persistent legends about a ghost - reportedly connected to an Ancient Egyptian mummy in the museum above. No credible sighting has ever been documented, but the story has circulated since the station closed and has proved durable enough to appear in print repeatedly.

russell-square.history: Opened on 15 December 1906 as part of the original Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway between Finsbury Park and Hammersmith. Designed by Leslie Green in his distinctive Edwardian Baroque style, featuring the iconic oxblood red faience facade with arcaded bays and Diocletian windows. The station gained Grade II listed status in 2011. On 7 July 2005, a bomb exploded on a train between King's Cross and Russell Square, killing 26 people - nearly half of all fatalities in that day's coordinated attacks. A memorial plaque is installed at the station.
funFacts: Russell Square has no escalators - passengers reach the platforms via three lifts or a spiral staircase with 175 steps, equivalent to a 15-storey building.
On 7 July 2005, the bomb that exploded between King's Cross and Russell Square killed 26 people, making it the deadliest of the four 7/7 attacks.
Each Leslie Green station on the Piccadilly line had its own unique tile colour scheme on the platforms, helping passengers identify their stop before literacy was universal.

down-street.history: Opened on 15 March 1907 between Dover Street (now Green Park) and Hyde Park Corner, Down Street was never a busy station. Its wealthy Mayfair neighbours preferred taxis, and the two flanking stations were only 500 metres away. Trains began skipping it regularly and it closed on 21 May 1932. Its afterlife proved more dramatic than its operating life: in 1939 the tunnels were converted into a secret underground bunker for the Railway Executive Committee. Winston Churchill used it as a shelter and meeting space for the War Cabinet during the Blitz, before the Cabinet War Rooms were ready. Churchill reportedly called it 'the barn'.
funFacts: Winston Churchill used Down Street as his secret wartime bunker during the Blitz, calling it 'the barn'.
The station was so poorly used that trains were already skipping it by 1918, just eleven years after it opened.
Evidence of Down Street can still be seen from passing trains - a stretch of beige brickwork between Green Park and Hyde Park Corner marks where the platforms once were.

york-road.history: Opened on 15 December 1906 as part of the original Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway, York Road sat between King's Cross and Caledonian Road in an industrial area that generated little passenger demand. Like Down Street, it was identified for closure as part of plans to speed up journey times on the extended Piccadilly line and closed on 19 September 1932 - the same day the first phase of the northern extension opened to Arnos Grove. The Leslie Green oxblood tiled building survives and is considered one of the most intact ghost station facades in London.
funFacts: York Road closed on the exact same day the Piccadilly line's northern extension to Arnos Grove opened - 19 September 1932.
The station's Leslie Green façade is considered one of the best-preserved ghost station exteriors on the entire Underground network.
Proposals to reopen York Road as part of the King's Cross regeneration have been floated but never pursued, partly due to an estimated £21 million renovation cost.

aldwych.history: Opened on 30 November 1907 as 'Strand' station, Aldwych was originally planned as the southern terminus of the Great Northern and Strand Railway before the merger that created the Piccadilly line left it as a dead-end spur from Holborn. It was renamed Aldwych on 9 May 1915. Never heavily used, the branch operated as a peak-hours shuttle from 1962. During both World Wars, the tunnels stored priceless artworks including the Elgin Marbles from the British Museum. The station finally closed on 30 September 1994 when the cost of replacing the original 1907 lifts - £3 million - was deemed unjustifiable for a station seeing only 450 passengers a day. It remains preserved and is a popular filming location.
funFacts: The Elgin Marbles were stored in Aldwych's tunnels during World War II for safekeeping.
Aldwych has appeared in dozens of films and TV productions since closure, including V for Vendetta, 28 Weeks Later, Atonement, and Sherlock.
The station still has its original 1907 lifts - the very ones whose £3 million replacement cost triggered its closure.

chancery-lane.history: Chancery Lane opened on 30 July 1900 on the Central London Railway, serving the legal heart of London - the Inns of Court, the Law Courts on the Strand, and Lincoln's Inn are all within walking distance. During the Second World War the deep-level section below the station was requisitioned as a secure storage and communications facility. The Public Record Office nearby used underground vaults to protect England's most important historical documents from bombing.

st-pauls.history: St Paul's opened on 30 July 1900 as Post Office on the Central London Railway, renamed St Paul's in 1937 after the Postmaster General objected to the station name conflating the railway with the postal service. The station sits directly beneath Christopher Wren's cathedral, completed in 1710 after the Great Fire of London. During the Blitz the survival of the dome became a symbol of national endurance - the photograph taken on 29 December 1940 of the dome visible through smoke from surrounding fires is one of the most iconic images of the Second World War.
funFacts: The cathedral's survival during the Blitz was partly deliberate: the Dean and Chapter organised a team of volunteers to watch the roof every night and extinguish incendiary bombs before they could take hold. On the night of 29 December 1940 - when 28 incendiaries hit the cathedral - the team prevented every fire from spreading.

bank.history: Bank opened on 30 July 1900 as 'City' on the Central London Railway - renamed Bank in 1940 - in the heart of London's financial district. The Northern line had already been serving the station since 1900 via the City and South London Railway, and the Waterloo & City line (as 'City') since 1898. Monument station, opened by the District and Metropolitan railways in 1884, sits adjacent and is connected by a long underground passage, making Bank-Monument one of the most complex interchange points on the network. The DLR added a station in 1991 and the Elizabeth line opened a new entrance nearby in 2022, further expanding what is already one of the deepest and most labyrinthine stations on the Underground.
funFacts: The Bank of England's gold vaults sit directly beneath the station - the bullion store holds around 400,000 gold bars and lies just metres from the Northern line tunnels running below Threadneedle Street.

The passage connecting Bank and Monument is long enough that TfL counts the interchange as equivalent to a surface connection - the walk between platforms takes around 10 minutes and is listed as such in journey planning.

regent-s-park.history: Regent's Park opened on 10 March 1906 on the Baker Street and Waterloo Railway. It is one of the quieter central London stations, serving the southern edge of the park and the Portland Place and Harley Street area. The BBC's Broadcasting House on Portland Place - the corporation's first purpose-built headquarters, completed in 1932 - is a short walk from the station exit. The park itself, designed by John Nash in the 1810s, contains London Zoo and an open-air theatre.

piccadilly-circus.history: Piccadilly Circus station opened in 1906 with both Bakerloo and Piccadilly line platforms, and was radically rebuilt in 1928 when Charles Holden created an enormous circular underground ticket hall directly beneath the road junction - accessed from street level by a ring of kiosks, one of the first such designs in the world. The solution managed the exceptionally high passenger flows at one of London's most congested junctions. The statue above the main exit - widely known as Eros but correctly depicting Anteros, god of selfless love - was erected in 1893 in memory of the philanthropist Lord Shaftesbury. The station is one of the busiest on the network on Friday and Saturday evenings, serving the restaurants, theatres, and clubs of the West End.
funFacts: Holden's 1928 ticket hall solved a specific problem: exits from the original station deposited passengers in the middle of the road junction with no clear pedestrian routes. The circular concourse - with exits feeding directly onto pavement edges on all sides - became an early model for managing large underground passenger flows at surface-level junctions.

The Anteros statue is made of aluminium - it was the first public memorial in London to use the metal, then experimental and expensive. The lightweight aluminium allowed the archer figure to be posed at an angle that would have been structurally impossible in bronze or stone.

waterloo.history: Waterloo tube station opened on 10 March 1906 on the Baker Street and Waterloo Railway - the line that gave the Bakerloo its name. The Northern line arrived in 1926 and the Jubilee line in 1999, making it a three-line interchange and one of the busiest on the network. The station sits beneath Waterloo mainline terminus, which handles more passengers than any other station in Britain; the two are so closely linked that their peak flows reinforce each other throughout the day. The Waterloo & City line - running to Bank since 1898 and operated by the Southern Railway and then British Rail before transfer to London Underground in 1994 - has its own dedicated platforms at a separate entrance.
funFacts: Waterloo mainline station was the London terminus for Eurostar services from their launch in 1994 until 2007, when international trains transferred to St. Pancras. The purpose-built Waterloo International terminal sat largely unused for years afterwards before being converted back to domestic use.
The last scheduled steam-hauled passenger train to depart a London terminus ran from Waterloo on 9 July 1967, marking the end of an era on British main lines.

lambeth-north.history: Opened on 10 March 1906 as Kennington Road on the Baker Street and Waterloo Railway, renamed Westminster Bridge Road in August 1906 and Lambeth North in 1917. It is one of the few tube stations with no interchange with any other line.

elephant-castle.history: Elephant and Castle has two tube stations: the Northern line (City and South London Railway) arrived in 1890, and the Bakerloo line opened its southern terminus here on 5 August 1906. The area's name comes from a pub that stood at the road junction - the pub's sign depicted a heraldic elephant and castle, a device associated with the Worshipful Company of Cutlers, whose trade in ivory gave the image its meaning. The surrounding area was heavily bombed during the Second World War and redeveloped in the 1960s, leaving a large, traffic-dominated junction that has defined the neighbourhood ever since. Significant regeneration of the area began in the 2010s and is ongoing.
funFacts: Charlie Chaplin was born in 1889 in nearby East Street, and Michael Faraday - pioneer of electromagnetism - was born in Newington Butts, a short walk from the station. The area claims two of the most consequential figures of their respective centuries.

barons-court.history: Opened on 9 October 1905 on the District Railway before the Piccadilly line began using the station on 15 December 1906. One of the few stations to open before the line it is now associated with.

west-kensington.history: Opened on 9 September 1874 on the District Railway as North End (Fulham), renamed West Kensington in 1877. A District-only station, it sits between Barons Court and Earl's Court where the Piccadilly line runs through on a parallel track without stopping.

earls-court.history: Opened on 30 October 1871 on the District Railway. It became a major junction when the Piccadilly line began using the station in 1906. The station served as a test bed for the Underground's first escalators in 1911 - a Seeberger machine running between the booking hall and the platforms, put into passenger service on 4 October that year, which represented a fundamental change in how high-volume Underground stations could be operated by removing their dependence on lift capacity.
funFacts: When Earl's Court's first escalator opened in 1911 there was widespread public anxiety about stepping onto a moving staircase. According to long-running London folklore the Underground hired a one-legged man, William 'Bumper' Harris, to ride the escalator up and down all day demonstrating that even with a wooden leg it was safe to use; Harris was a real Underground employee at Earl's Court, although historians dispute whether the famous demonstration story is fact or apocrypha.

knightsbridge.history: Knightsbridge opened on 15 December 1906 on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway, serving one of London's most expensive shopping districts. Harrods - established on Brompton Road in 1849 and now occupying nearly five acres - has its main entrance a short walk from the station. The surrounding streets include Harvey Nichols and a concentration of international luxury brand flagship stores that make Knightsbridge one of the most valuable retail locations in the world.

hyde-park-corner.history: Hyde Park Corner opened on 15 December 1906 on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway. The station sits at the southeastern corner of Hyde Park, adjacent to Wellington Arch and Apsley House - the Iron Duke's London residence, now a museum. The junction above is ringed by war memorials including the Australian, New Zealand, and Machine Gun Corps memorials, making Hyde Park Corner one of the most significant memorial sites in London.

green-park.history: Green Park opened on 15 December 1906 as Dover Street on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway, renamed Green Park in 1933. It became a three-line interchange with the Victoria line in 1969 and the Jubilee line in 1979, giving it direct connections to much of central London. The station serves Buckingham Palace, the Ritz Hotel, and St James's Street to the south - one of the most concentrated areas of royal connections and historic gentlemen's clubs in London.
funFacts: The original Dover Street station building - a Leslie Green ox-blood terracotta design from 1906 - still stands on Dover Street, now converted to commercial use and Grade II listed. When the station was renamed Green Park in 1933, the surface building became redundant as the main entrance; the Green Park name refers to the park across Piccadilly, not the street.

leicester-square.history: Leicester Square opened on 15 December 1906 on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway. The station serves the heart of London's cinema and theatreland district - the surrounding streets host major film premieres and some of London's busiest West End theatres. The station is consistently among the busiest on the network on Friday and Saturday nights when the entertainment district reaches peak capacity.
funFacts: Leicester Square station relies entirely on lifts rather than escalators - the station was built under an already densely developed area in 1906, leaving no room for the longer footprint escalators require. The lift shafts at 24 metres are among the deepest on the Piccadilly line.

covent-garden.history: Opened on 11 April 1907 on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway, Covent Garden was designed by Leslie Green in his distinctive ox-blood glazed terracotta style and is one of the best-preserved of his original surface buildings. The station lies just 260 metres from Leicester Square - a distance TfL's own maps acknowledge as walkable - and has at various times been considered for closure on those grounds. It has only two lifts connecting the ticket hall to the platforms, with no escalators, and on summer afternoons regularly reaches capacity, with staff managing queues on the street outside. The station serves the Covent Garden piazza and its surrounding theatres and tourist attractions, making it one of the busiest small-capacity stations on the network.
funFacts: Covent Garden is reputed to be one of the most haunted stations on the Underground. The ghost most often reported - a tall Edwardian man in a top hat - has been connected by station folklore to actor William Terriss, who was stabbed outside the nearby Adelphi Theatre in 1897. Staff reported sightings as recently as 1955.

The station was seriously considered for closure in 1994 on the grounds that its proximity to Leicester Square made it economically unjustifiable. The proposal was reversed following public opposition - a pattern that has recurred whenever the idea has been raised since.

caledonian-road.history: Opened on 15 December 1906 on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway. The station serves the Caledonian Road area of Islington and retains its original Leslie Green ox-blood terracotta facade, one of the most complete surviving examples of his deep-tube house style on the Piccadilly line. The building was Grade II listed for its architectural significance.

holloway-road.history: Opened on 15 December 1906 on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway. The station retains its original Leslie Green ox-blood faience facade and serves the busy Holloway Road commercial district in north London. The building was Grade II listed for its architectural significance.
funFacts: Holloway Road was the site of one of the strangest experiments in Underground history. In 1906 the American engineer Jesse Reno installed a prototype spiral escalator inside one of the station's lift shafts - a continuous helical conveyor that wound its way down through the building - but the device was tested and never opened to passengers, with the operator opting instead for conventional lifts. The Underground's first working escalator finally arrived five years later at Earl's Court.

arsenal.history: Opened on 15 December 1906 as Gillespie Road on the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway. Renamed Arsenal in 1932 at the request of Herbert Chapman, manager of Arsenal FC, making it the only London Underground station named after a football club.

finsbury-park.history: Opened on 15 December 1906 as the northern terminus of the Great Northern, Piccadilly and Brompton Railway - the company that became the Piccadilly line. The station was designed by Leslie Green, whose ox-blood red terracotta facades became the defining look of the early Piccadilly and Bakerloo lines. The Victoria line arrived in 1968, transforming Finsbury Park into a major interchange with connections to National Rail services on the East Coast main line. It remains one of the busiest stations outside Zone 1.
funFacts: Leslie Green, who designed Finsbury Park and over 50 other tube stations, died in 1908 aged just 33 - he completed the designs before tuberculosis prevented him from seeing most of them built.

golders-green.history: Opened on 22 June 1907 as the northern terminus of the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway. The station was built above ground with an open-air depot alongside, and became one of the first Underground termini to generate significant suburban housing development around it. The CCE&HR - one of three Yerkes-funded UERL tubes opened in the great burst of 1906-07 and the last of them to enter service - had deliberately driven its new line out beyond the existing built-up edge of London, betting that the railway itself would create the suburb it nominally served. When trains first ran in June 1907 there were no streets and barely any houses around the station, and the Underground Group's directors had been openly mocked in the press for terminating their new tube in open country; within fifteen years Golders Green was a major residential area, and within twenty it had been absorbed into the continuous outward sprawl of north-west London. The model - drive a deep-tube line into farmland and let the housing follow - went on to define the Edgware extension of 1923-24 and the Stanmore, Cockfosters and Morden extensions in the decade after that. The original 1907 station was modest in scale; the 1923 extension north to Hendon Central and Edgware brought a substantial rebuild by Stanley Heaps, the Underground Group's chief architect, to handle the heavier traffic, adding the parade of shops and the large bus interchange that still front the station today. The adjoining Golders Green Depot opened on the same site in 1907 and remains the major stabling and maintenance facility for the Charing Cross branch of the Northern line, with trains routinely terminating at Golders Green to enter it.
funFacts: When Golders Green opened in 1907 it served almost no one. The land around the station had no street network and no electricity supply, and the area's pre-tube population was numbered in the low hundreds. Within a generation it had become one of the most densely built-up suburbs in north London - the textbook proof of concept for the 'run the tube into open country and let the housing follow' strategy that would later define the Edgware, Stanmore and Cockfosters extensions.

Directly opposite the station stands the Golders Green Hippodrome, a 3,000-seat music hall opened in 1913 specifically to capitalise on the new commuter traffic the tube had brought. From 1968 onward the BBC used the hall as a major recording venue for orchestral and light-music broadcasts - the BBC Concert Orchestra was effectively based there for decades - before the building passed to other uses in the 2000s.

hampstead.history: Hampstead opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway, serving the hilltop village that had long resisted railways. The steep topography of Hampstead Heath forced the line deep: at 58.5 metres below street level, Hampstead is the deepest station on the London Underground. The depth made escalators impractical from the outset and the station was built with lifts, the original 1907 shafts still in daily use. The Leslie Green building at street level sits quietly on the High Street with little to suggest the near-20-storey descent behind it. During the Second World War the platforms sheltered up to 5,000 people per night.
funFacts: At 58.5 metres below street level, Hampstead is the deepest station on the London Underground - so deep that during the Second World War its platforms sheltered up to 5,000 people per night from German bombing raids.
Because of its depth, Hampstead still relies on lifts rather than escalators; the original 1907 lift shafts remain in use, and the journey from street to platform takes over a minute.

Hampstead has among the lowest weekday passenger numbers of any central London tube station - the affluent village above is walkable and well-served by buses - yet its depth and Leslie Green facade make it a favourite with photographers and urban explorers.

belsize-park.history: Opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway. The station served as a deep air-raid shelter during the Second World War, accommodating thousands of Londoners on its platforms each night. From 1942 a separate dedicated deep-level shelter was excavated beneath the existing station - one of eight such shelters built under London tube stations - capable of holding 8,000 people in bunk beds across two parallel tunnels. The Leslie Green street-level building survives largely intact and is Grade II listed.
funFacts: The deep-level shelter under Belsize Park found an unexpected post-war use in June 1948, when it was opened as temporary accommodation for several hundred Caribbean migrants who had just arrived on the Empire Windrush at Tilbury and had nowhere else to stay. The nearby Brixton labour exchange directed many of the new arrivals to register for work, and the Windrush generation's first nights in Britain were spent in the converted air-raid tunnels deep beneath the Northern line.

chalk-farm.history: Opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway. The station's name derives from a corruption of 'Chalcot Farm', a local manor that once occupied the site. It retains its original ox-blood tiled Leslie Green facade.

camden-town.history: Camden Town opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway, its facade on Camden High Street one of Leslie Green's finest surviving ox-blood terracotta buildings. The station's importance grew in 1924 when the CCEHR and City & South London Railway merged to create the Northern line: at Camden Town, trains from the south arrive via two branches (Charing Cross and Bank) and depart north to two destinations (Edgware and High Barnet) - four possible journeys from a single stop. The area draws over 100,000 visitors on peak weekends, regularly forcing the station to close its street exits to manage the crowds.
funFacts: Camden Town sits at the Northern line's most complex junction: heading north, trains split for the Edgware and High Barnet branches; heading south, they diverge again for Bank and Charing Cross - four possible journeys from a single interchange.

The station regularly closes its street exits on busy weekend afternoons to manage overcrowding from Camden Market, which draws over 100,000 visitors on peak Saturdays - one of only a handful of stations in London to do this routinely.

The ox-blood glazed terracotta facade on Camden High Street is one of Leslie Green's best-preserved station buildings from 1907, its distinctive deep-red tiles and steel-framed upper storeys largely unchanged from opening day.

mornington-crescent.history: Opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway. The station closed between 1992 and 1998 for lift refurbishment, during which it became the unlikely subject of a long-running BBC Radio 4 comedy segment.
funFacts: The Mornington Crescent game on 'I'm Sorry I Haven't a Clue' has no actual rules. Panellists take turns naming London Underground stations in apparently elaborate sequences governed by invented strategies and ever-shifting variant playbooks, all of it pure improvisation - the long-running joke being that the audience and players collectively pretend an enormously complicated rule-system governs play. The game first aired in 1978; the station's 1998 reopening, after almost six years of closure, was celebrated on-air with a special edition.

euston.history: Euston tube station opened on 12 May 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway, tunnelled beneath one of Britain's largest mainline termini while it kept operating above. The City & South London Railway added platforms in 1914, completing the Northern line arrangement that persists today. The Victoria line arrived in 1968, and its Euston platforms were among the first in the world to use automatic train operation - trains ran to a precise timetable with drivers present but not controlling speed between stations. The station sits below the main departure point for services to Birmingham, Manchester, Liverpool, and Glasgow.
funFacts: Euston tube station opened on 12 May 1907, with engineers tunnelling through ground just metres below the foundations of one of Britain's busiest mainline termini while the station above kept running.

The Victoria line platforms at Euston, opened in 1968, were among the first in the world to use fully automatic train operation - trains ran to a precise timetable with drivers present but not controlling speed or braking between stations.

Euston mainline station above is the London terminus for services to Birmingham, Manchester, Liverpool, and Glasgow, making the tube platforms below a constant crossroads between the national rail network and the Underground.

angel.history: Angel opened on 17 November 1901 on the City & South London Railway's extension from Moorgate, one of the earliest deep electric tube railways in the world. It takes its name from the Angel Inn, a coaching inn at the junction of Islington High Street and City Road since at least the 17th century. The original CSLR tunnels were narrower than later Underground standards and the entire line had to be enlarged in 1922-24. In the early 1990s the station was rebuilt to handle growing passenger numbers: the old lifts were replaced with an escalator 60 metres long - the longest on the network - bored alongside the existing tunnels while trains ran below.
funFacts: Angel's escalators, installed during a major rebuild in 1992, are the longest on the London Underground at 60 metres - the height of a 15-storey building - carrying passengers from street level to the deep Northern line platforms.

The station takes its name from the Angel Inn, a coaching inn that stood at the junction above from at least the 17th century and was a well-known stop on the Great North Road long before the railway arrived.

When the City & South London Railway opened here in 1901, passengers rode in small windowless carriages nicknamed 'padded cells' - with no way to see outside, they relied entirely on platform staff calling out the station name.

old-street.history: Opened on 17 November 1901 on the City & South London Railway, Old Street sits at one of east London's busiest road junctions. The roundabout above became informally known as Silicon Roundabout in the 2010s as the surrounding area developed into London's primary technology and startup cluster, housing hundreds of digital and creative businesses. The Northern line platforms are deep underground; surface-level National Rail services use an adjacent station on Old Street itself.
funFacts: Bunhill Fields, a five-minute walk from the station, contains the graves of William Blake, Daniel Defoe, and John Bunyan - making the neighbourhood one of the most significant sites of English nonconformist and literary history.

Before the tech cluster arrived, the streets around Old Street were a centre of furniture manufacturing - workshops along Curtain Road and Shoreditch supplied London's homes from the Victorian era through to the 1960s, when cheaper imports began to displace the trade.

warren-street.history: Warren Street opened on 22 June 1907 as 'Euston Road' on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway, one of twelve stations that began service on the same day. The name changed to Warren Street in 1908 to avoid confusion with Euston station nearby. It sits in Fitzrovia, between Bloomsbury and Marylebone, close to University College Hospital and several medical research institutions. The Victoria line platforms were added in 1968. On 21 July 2005, two weeks after the 7 July bombings, a bomb on a Victoria line train here failed to detonate - one of four coordinated but unsuccessful attacks that day.
funFacts: Warren Street opened on 22 June 1907 as 'Euston Road' on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway - renamed Warren Street in 1908 to avoid confusion with Euston station nearby.

The BT Tower, visible from the station exit, opened in 1965 as the Post Office Tower and was briefly the tallest building in the UK at 177 metres - Warren Street was the nearest tube stop to what Londoners then considered the most conspicuous symbol of the modern age.

The station sits adjacent to University College Hospital and a cluster of medical research institutions, making it one of the busiest stations on the Victoria line outside the central tourist corridor.

goodge-street.history: Goodge Street opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway, one of twelve stations that began service the same day. During the Second World War the deep-level section below the station was requisitioned as a military communications centre for US forces: General Dwight Eisenhower established his advance London headquarters here in 1942 while planning the Allied landings in North Africa. The tunnels, around 30 metres below street level, were secure from the bombing hitting the city above. The deep shelter was later used for early planning stages of Operation Overlord - the D-Day landings.
funFacts: Goodge Street's deep-level tunnels were among several used by Allied forces for secure communications during the war. Their depth - beyond the reach of any bomb the Luftwaffe deployed over London - made them ideal for equipment that could not be risked in buildings above ground.

The BT Tower, visible from the station exit, opened in 1965 as the Post Office Tower and was briefly the tallest building in the UK at 177 metres. Warren Street was the nearest tube stop, but Goodge Street was also within easy walking distance of what Londoners then considered the most conspicuous symbol of the modern age.

charing-cross.history: The site that is now Charing Cross tube station was served as 'Trafalgar Square' by the Bakerloo line from 1906 and the Northern line from 1907 - the Northern line's platforms here were initially called 'Strand' before being renamed Charing Cross in 1915. The station's current name and configuration date from 1 November 1979, when the Jubilee line opened its original southern terminus here and the entire complex was renamed Charing Cross. That terminus closed in 1999 when the Jubilee line was extended eastwards to Stratford; trains now pass through without stopping. The station sits beside Trafalgar Square, adjacent to the National Gallery and the approaches to Whitehall.
funFacts: When the Jubilee line was extended in 1999, its platforms at Charing Cross were simply sealed off rather than demolished. The closed tunnels and platforms still exist beneath the station and are regularly used as a filming location - they have appeared in films including 28 Days Later and numerous television productions.

kennington.history: Opened on 18 December 1890 as one of the original stations on the City & South London Railway - the world's first deep-level electric tube railway. Running beneath the Thames with electric locomotives, the line was a radical departure from the steam-hauled underground railways of the era. The early carriages were nicknamed 'padded cells': windowless wooden boxes where passengers sat back-to-back with only a dim electric bulb overhead. The line was substantially rebuilt in the 1920s to widen its Victorian tunnels to standard tube dimensions. Beneath Kennington lies a reversing loop that allows southbound trains to turn back north - a feature unique on the Underground, still in active daily use.
funFacts: The City & South London Railway was the first in the world to use electric traction throughout from opening - its 1890 debut proved to sceptical engineers everywhere that electric railways could work beneath city streets.

The Kennington loop, built during the 1920s reconstruction, is the only underground turning loop of its kind still in regular use on the network - trains on the Bank branch use it to reverse direction without occupying a through platform.

oval.history: Opened on 18 December 1890 on the City & South London Railway. Named after the nearby Kia Oval cricket ground, home of Surrey County Cricket Club. The station is the closest Underground station to the ground, which has hosted Test cricket since 1880.

stockwell.history: Stockwell opened on 18 December 1890 as one of the five original stations on the City & South London Railway - the world's first deep-level electric underground railway. The cylindrical tunnels were just 10 feet in diameter and the windowless carriages earned the line the nickname 'sardine box railway'. The Victoria line added a second level beneath in 1971, making Stockwell the first major interchange south of the river on the new line. On 22 July 2005 Jean Charles de Menezes, an unarmed Brazilian electrician, was shot and killed at the station by Metropolitan Police officers who had mistaken him for a terrorism suspect - an incident that led to significant changes in police firearms procedures.
funFacts: The City & South London Railway used separate electric locomotives to haul three passenger cars at a time through the tunnels, rather than self-propelled trains. When the line was modernised and the tunnels enlarged in the 1920s, the original rolling stock was scrapped entirely - it was simply too small to adapt to standard Underground dimensions.

manor-house.history: Opened on 19 September 1932 as part of the Piccadilly line's extension north from Finsbury Park. Named after a long-demolished manor house in the area, the station was built to serve a rapidly expanding suburban neighbourhood and is located close to Finsbury Park itself. Charles Holden's design placed the entire ticket hall below the busy junction of Seven Sisters Road and Green Lanes, accessed via a network of subway entrances spread around the intersection, and the building was Grade II listed in 1994.
funFacts: Holden's design at Manor House treated the surface junction itself as part of the station.
