The ticket hall sits entirely below ground, and pedestrians can cross between the surface bus stops, the local shops and the Underground via the subway network without ever reaching street level - a piece of integrated transport planning more characteristic of mid-twentieth-century continental practice than of 1930s London.

turnpike-lane.history: Opened on 19 September 1932 on the Piccadilly line extension. The station was designed by Charles Holden in his distinctive modernist brick style, which was inspired by visits to Scandinavian architecture and became the template for many Underground stations of the period. A tall illuminated brick pylon rising from the corner of the site acted as a beacon to attract passengers from Wood Green High Road, and the station was Grade II listed in 1994.

wood-green.history: Opened on 19 September 1932 as part of the Piccadilly line's northern extension. Designed by Charles Holden, the station was part of a celebrated sequence of modernist brick buildings that the architectural press praised as the finest public architecture of the interwar period. The site, on a sharp corner where Lordship Lane meets the High Road, prompted Holden to wrap the brick frontage in a continuous curved sweep around the junction - a solution dictated by the street geometry but characteristic of his willingness to let local conditions reshape the standard plan. The station was Grade II listed in 1994.

bounds-green.history: Opened on 19 September 1932 on the Piccadilly line extension. The station was designed by Charles Holden in the modernist brick idiom of the rest of the 1932 extension and is distinguished externally by its octagonal brick ticket-hall pavilion. The below-ground booking hall was used as an air-raid shelter during the Blitz, and on 13 October 1940 a German bomb penetrated to the platform, killing nineteen people sheltering below. The station was Grade II listed in 1994.

arnos-grove.history: Opened on 19 September 1932 on the Piccadilly line's extension into north London, Arnos Grove was designed by Charles Holden following a study tour he undertook with Frank Pick - the Underground Group's commercial director - through Scandinavia and the Netherlands in 1930. The circular drum of the booking hall, lit by a ring of windows beneath a flat concrete roof, was inspired by Swedish civic modernism. Holden considered it among his finest works. The building was listed Grade II* in 1971 and upgraded to Grade I in 2011 - one of only a small number of 20th-century buildings in England to hold that designation. Its design influenced commercial and civic architecture across Britain throughout the 1930s.
funFacts: Holden and Pick's 1930 study tour covered Scandinavia, the Netherlands, and Germany over three weeks, visiting work including Erik Gunnar Asplund's Stockholm Public Library (completed 1928), whose circular reading room is a direct precedent for Arnos Grove's drum booking hall. The journey shaped every station Holden designed for the Piccadilly extension.

Arnos Grove's upgrade to Grade I listed in 2011 placed it among the 2% of all listed buildings in England that hold the top designation - in the same category as the Palace of Westminster, St Paul's Cathedral, and the Tower of London.

southgate.history: Opened on 13 March 1933 as the Piccadilly line reached its penultimate stop before Cockfosters, Southgate is considered one of Charles Holden's most elegant station designs. The circular glazed canopy over the street-level island entrance - a ring of metal columns supporting a flat concrete disc with a continuous band of glazing - was designed to be illuminated from within at night, creating a glowing civic landmark for the new suburbs spreading north along the Great Cambridge Road. The station is Grade II* listed. All six of Holden's stations on the Cockfosters extension were built within a coherent architectural language, making the outer Piccadilly line one of the most unified stretches of public architecture built in Britain between the wars.
funFacts: The canopy was specifically designed to be legible from a moving vehicle - Holden believed passengers should be able to identify a tube station instantly from the road. The illuminated drum at night achieved this in a way the conventional street-frontage entrance could not.

Southgate's circular entrance sits on a traffic island in the centre of a roundabout, meaning passengers approach from any direction and cross the road to enter. The roundabout was laid out to complement the station rather than the other way around - an unusually deliberate approach to the relationship between transport and street design.

oakwood.history: Opened on 13 March 1933 as Enfield West on the Piccadilly line, renamed Oakwood in 1946. One of Charles Holden's most acclaimed station designs, it is set in a cutting and features a long brick building with a flat roof used as a bus station above - an unusually direct integration of tube and surface transport for the period. The station was Grade II listed for its architectural significance.

cockfosters.history: Opened on 31 July 1933 as the northern terminus of the Piccadilly line extension. Designed by Charles Holden, the station is built at ground level with a dramatic concrete umbrella canopy over the platforms. It sits on the edge of Enfield Chase and remains the line's northern terminus today.

borough.history: Borough opened on 18 December 1890 on the City and South London Railway, the world's first deep-level electric railway. It is one of the least-altered stations on the Underground: the original Victorian tilework survives largely intact, the platforms retain the cramped scale of the 1890 design, and little about the station suggests 130 years of change above ground. The deep-level tunnels running north to London Bridge are among the oldest tube tunnels in continuous use anywhere in the world. The station serves Borough Market and the streets of Bermondsey, and its unhurried atmosphere is unusual for a station so close to the centre of the city.
funFacts: The tunnels beneath Borough were bored using a Greathead Shield - a tunnelling device developed specifically for this project that became the template for soft-ground tunnelling worldwide. Variants of the same basic design were still in use during construction of Crossrail over a century later.

london-bridge.history: London Bridge tube station opened on 25 December 1890 on the City and South London Railway - the world's first deep-level electric railway - its Christmas Day opening a reflection of the Victorian era's different relationship with the working week. The station sits beneath one of London's oldest mainline termini, with trains on that site dating back to 1836. The Jubilee line extension arrived in 1999, substantially enlarging the underground station and improving connections to Canary Wharf and the south-east. The surrounding area has changed dramatically since the 1990s, with the construction of The Shard in 2012 and the complete reconstruction of the mainline station above.
funFacts: The City and South London Railway charged a flat fare of 2d regardless of distance - a deliberate policy to make the railway accessible to working-class passengers rather than pricing by zone as later lines would do.

London Bridge mainline station is the oldest railway terminus in London still in regular use, with the original 1836 terminus of the London and Greenwich Railway on this site - over 50 years before the tube arrived beneath it.

walthamstow-central.history: Opened in September 1968 as the northern terminus of the Victoria line, a position it still holds today. The station shares an interchange with the London Overground's Chingford line, connecting the Victoria line with the historic market town centre of Walthamstow.

blackhorse-road.history: Opened in September 1968 as part of the Victoria line's original northern section, serving the Blackhorse Lane area of Walthamstow. The station forms an interchange with London Overground's Gospel Oak to Barking line, providing connections across the north-east of the city.

tottenham-hale.history: Opened in September 1968 as part of the Victoria line's original northern section. The station has since grown into a significant interchange, with National Rail services to Stansted Airport and Cambridge via the Lea Valley line departing from the adjacent mainline platforms.

seven-sisters.history: Opened in September 1968 as part of the Victoria line's original northern section. The name derives from a local landmark documented in Tottenham since the 17th century: seven elms planted in a ring, traditionally said to mark a spot where seven sisters parted ways. The trees have been felled and replanted several times as they aged - the current circle dates from 1996. The station connects with National Rail's Lea Valley lines and is one of the Victoria line's busiest outer stations, serving a densely populated and historically diverse area of north London.
funFacts: The seven elm trees are one of north London's most persistently replanted landmarks - on at least four occasions the ageing trees have been replaced while preserving the circle, making them a living piece of local continuity rather than a fixed historical object.

highbury-islington.history: Opened in September 1968 as the original southern terminus of the Victoria line - the first new deep tube line built in London for over 60 years. Extended south to Victoria in December of the same year, it became a through-station and one of north London's busiest interchange points, connecting the Victoria line with London Overground services on the North London line and National Rail. Arsenal's historic Highbury stadium operated a short walk away from 1913 until the club's move to the Emirates in 2006.
funFacts: The Victoria line, inaugurated at Highbury & Islington, was the first Underground line to use automatic train operation from the outset - drivers were on board but the trains ran under computer control, a first for a major metro system.

pimlico.history: Opened in September 1972, Pimlico was inserted as an intermediate station on the Victoria line over a year after trains had already been running through the site on the Brixton extension. It is the only Victoria line station added retrospectively to an already-operating section of the line, serving the residential Pimlico area near the Tate Britain gallery.

vauxhall.history: Opened in July 1971 as part of the Victoria line's Brixton extension. The station is a key interchange with National Rail services and sits near Vauxhall Cross, the riverside headquarters of MI6 - a striking postmodern building opened in 1994 that became recognisable worldwide through the James Bond film franchise.
funFacts: The Russian word for a major railway terminus, 'vokzal' (вокзал), is widely held to derive from Vauxhall itself. According to the popular etymology a nineteenth-century Russian delegation visited either the Vauxhall Pleasure Gardens or the early Vauxhall railway station in south London, took the name back with them and applied it to St Petersburg's Tsarskoye Selo terminus - from which it spread through Russian as the standard word for a large railway station. Linguists are not unanimous on the story's accuracy, but the connection is widely repeated.

brixton.history: Opened on 23 July 1971 as the southern terminus of the Victoria line and the final section of a route first planned in 1943. The station sits at the heart of Brixton, one of south London's most culturally significant districts - home to a large Caribbean community from the late 1940s onwards and the focal point of the 1981 Brixton uprising, a pivotal event that prompted the Scarman Report and significant changes in British policing. Extensions further south to Streatham or Tooting were surveyed and costed in the 1970s but shelved after funding was cut; Brixton has remained the terminus for over 50 years.
funFacts: Electric Avenue - a short walk from the station - was the first street in Britain to be lit by electricity, which is how it got its name. The covered market arcade opened in 1888 and is still trading today, making it one of the oldest surviving market streets in south London.

west-hampstead.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, previously served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1879. One of three West Hampstead stations in close proximity, it sits alongside Thameslink and Overground services. The 1879 Metropolitan Railway opening was part of the Met's extension from Swiss Cottage to Willesden Green, and the station has been continuously open ever since: it passed from the Met to the Bakerloo line with the Stanmore branch transfer of 1939, and from the Bakerloo to the Jubilee on the new line's inauguration in 1979 - the same physical tracks under three successive line liveries. The Thameslink and London Overground sister-stations were originally Midland Railway and North London Railway facilities respectively, making West Hampstead one of the densest single-name three-station clusters anywhere on the London network.

kilburn.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, having previously been served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1879 under the name Kilburn & Brondesbury - simplified to plain Kilburn in 1950. The earlier dual name reflected the historic boundary between the two neighbouring districts.

willesden-green.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, previously served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1879.

dollis-hill.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, previously served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1909.

neasden.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, previously served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1880.

wembley-park.history: Opened on 12 October 1894 on the Metropolitan Railway, Wembley Park owes much of its significance to Sir Edward Watkin, the Metropolitan's chairman, who acquired adjacent land and in 1892 began building a tower intended to surpass the Eiffel Tower. The project collapsed after completing only its first stage - the sodden ground and lack of funds brought it to a halt - and the rusting ironwork was demolished in 1907. The cleared site became the home of the British Empire Exhibition of 1924, for which Wembley Stadium was built. The Jubilee line began serving the station on 1 May 1979. On event days the station handles some of the largest and most concentrated passenger flows on the entire Underground.
funFacts: Watkin's Tower reached just one stage - a squat iron lattice base roughly 47 metres high - before the project was abandoned. The derelict structure stood for years before demolition in 1907, earning the nickname 'the London Stump'.

The Empire Stadium was built in just 300 days for the 1924 British Empire Exhibition, but hosted its first FA Cup final a year before the Exhibition opened. Unofficial estimates put the crowd at 200,000 - with thousands spilling onto the pitch before the match could start.

preston-road.history: Opened on 21 May 1908 as part of the Metropolitan Railway's extension serving the growing suburbs northwest of London.

northwick-park.history: Opened on 28 June 1923 as 'Northwick Park and Kenton' on the Metropolitan Railway, serving the developing residential areas of Harrow. Renamed Northwick Park on 15 March 1937.

kingsbury.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, previously served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1932.

queensbury.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, previously served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1934.

canons-park.history: Opened on 1 May 1979 on the Jubilee line, previously served by the Metropolitan Railway from 1932 as Canons Park (Edgware).

stanmore.history: Opened on 10 December 1932 by the Metropolitan Railway as the terminus of a new electric branch from Wembley Park, Stanmore was one of the last stations the Met built before the company was absorbed by the London Passenger Transport Board the following July. The Arts and Crafts station building - red brick, pitched tile roofs, tall chimneys - was designed by the Metropolitan's chief architect Charles Walter Clark in the deliberately domestic style he used across the Met's 1920s and 1930s suburban stations, intended to echo the model villages the company was simultaneously selling to commuters. The branch passed to the Bakerloo line on 20 November 1939, then to the Jubilee on 1 May 1979 when the new line absorbed the route north of Baker Street. Beyond the station, Stanmore Hill rises sharply toward Bentley Priory - the country house that served as RAF Fighter Command's headquarters during the Battle of Britain in 1940.
funFacts: Stanmore was the last brand-new station opened by the Metropolitan Railway. Just seven months after its December 1932 opening the company was absorbed into the London Passenger Transport Board, ending nearly 70 years of independent operation that had begun with the world's first underground railway in 1863.

Plans approved in the late 1930s would have extended the branch further north-west to Bushey Heath, with a major new depot at Aldenham. Construction began in 1939 but was halted by the Second World War, and post-war Green Belt restrictions killed the scheme entirely. The Aldenham site was eventually completed instead as London Transport's main bus overhaul works - one of the most ambitious never-completed Underground extensions.

southwark.history: Opened as part of the Jubilee line extension in 1999, Southwark station features a dramatic elliptical ticket hall designed by Richard MacCormac, widely regarded as one of the finest examples of contemporary station architecture on the network.

bermondsey.history: Opened in 1999 as part of the Jubilee line extension, Bermondsey serves a district historically known for its leather trade and food markets. The station brought rapid transit connections to an area previously served only by buses and the National Rail station at South Bermondsey - a short walk to the south on the line from London Bridge.

canada-water.history: Opened in 1999 and named after the former timber pond that once dominated the Rotherhithe peninsula, Canada Water serves as an interchange between the Jubilee line and the London Overground.

canary-wharf.history: Canary Wharf station opened on 17 September 1999 as part of the Jubilee line extension, built on the site of the former West India Docks. Norman Foster's design creates an enormous underground space - the station is around 290 metres long, one of the largest on the network - with the ticket hall sitting within an elliptical glass canopy set in the old dock basin. The Jubilee line extension transformed Docklands from an isolated development into a fully connected part of the city; Canary Wharf now ranks among the busiest stations on the Underground, carrying the financial district's workforce directly to Westminster and the City. The Elizabeth line added a second Canary Wharf station in 2022, accessed separately from the shopping centre above.
funFacts: The station was built inside the drained West India North Dock - the Victorian dock basin was temporarily dammed and excavated, and the concrete station box constructed within the exposed dock floor. The original 19th-century dock walls form part of the station's exterior structure today.

The glass canopy above the ticket hall has since been developed into a public garden known as Jubilee Park - an unusual outcome for a station roof, and one that makes Canary Wharf one of the few tube stations where the entrance sits beneath a public green space.

The Jubilee line extension cost approximately £3.5 billion - nearly three times the original 1990 estimate of £1.7 billion. Complex engineering at stations like Canary Wharf, requiring construction within the historic dock environment, was a major factor in the overrun.

north-greenwich.history: Opened in 1999 to serve the Millennium Dome - later the O2 arena - North Greenwich is one of the largest stations on the Underground, with a cavernous blue-tiled interior designed by Alsop and Störmer.

canning-town.history: Canning Town joined the Jubilee line in 1999, providing a key interchange with the Docklands Light Railway. The station serves a part of east London that has undergone significant regeneration since the extension opened.

west-ham.history: West Ham became part of the Jubilee line in 1999, creating an important interchange hub with District line and c2c services. The station sits at the gateway to the lower Lea Valley.

stratford.history: Stratford joined the tube network on 4 December 1946 when the Central line's post-war eastern extension opened. The Jubilee line extension arrived in November 1999, beginning a transformation that accelerated when Stratford was chosen as the gateway to the 2012 Olympic Park. The station now serves more lines than almost anywhere else on the network - Central, Jubilee, Elizabeth, DLR, Overground, and National Rail - and the surrounding Stratford City development has become one of east London's most ambitious regeneration projects.
funFacts: West Ham played at Upton Park less than a mile from Stratford for over a century. When they moved to the London Stadium in the Olympic Park in 2016, their home ground shifted directly above the station that had served their supporters for generations.

whitechapel.history: Whitechapel's Elizabeth line platforms opened in 2022 as part of the Crossrail project, bringing a major new east-west connection to a district long served only by the District and Hammersmith & City lines. The station sits at the heart of one of London's most historically layered neighbourhoods, known for its Victorian market, waves of immigration, and the notorious Jack the Ripper associations. The original station opened on 10 April 1876 as part of the East London Railway's northern extension to Shoreditch, running through Marc Brunel's Thames Tunnel - completed in 1843 as the first sub-river tunnel ever built and converted for trains in 1869 - and the District Railway began calling from 1884.
funFacts: Since the 2022 opening of the Elizabeth line, Whitechapel has had platforms on three distinct vertical levels - an unusual arrangement on the London Underground. The cut-and-cover District and Hammersmith & City platforms sit just below the street; the former East London Railway (now London Overground) platforms run beneath them in the 1869 brick-lined cutting that approaches Marc Brunel's Thames Tunnel; and the new Elizabeth line platforms sit deeper still, reached by long escalators that drop below both of the older stations stacked above.

custom-house.history: Custom House joined the Elizabeth line in 2022, having previously been served only by the Docklands Light Railway since 1994. The station takes its name from the nearby Royal Victoria Dock, once a hub of international trade. Its inclusion in Crossrail brought rapid central London connections to the Royal Docks regeneration area for the first time.

abbey-wood.history: Abbey Wood became the southeastern terminus of the Elizabeth line in 2022, ending decades of relative isolation for a suburb previously connected to central London only by slow Southeastern rail services. The new station building, designed by Weston Williamson and Partners, marked a dramatic upgrade from the old platforms and became a source of local pride.

farringdon-elizabeth.history: Farringdon - originally Farringdon Street - was the eastern terminus of the Metropolitan Railway on 10 January 1863, one of the two endpoints of the world's first underground railway journey. It sat at the foot of the Snow Hill tunnel, which from 1866 allowed through trains between the Metropolitan and the London, Chatham and Dover Railway south of the Thames. That link fell dormant for most of the 20th century until Thameslink restored it in 1988. The Elizabeth line's arrival in 2022 completed the transformation: Farringdon now sits at the intersection of two of London's most important cross-city routes.
funFacts: Farringdon - originally called Farringdon Street - was the eastern terminus of the Metropolitan Railway on 10 January 1863, one of the two endpoints of the world's first underground railway journey.

The station is now served by four lines (Metropolitan, Circle, Hammersmith & City, and Elizabeth), more than at any point in its 160-year history, with the Elizabeth line alone doubling the number of passengers passing through each day.

Smithfield Market, one of the largest wholesale meat markets in the world and active near this site since the 10th century, sits directly above the northern entrance - deliveries to its traders were once made via a dedicated underground siding from the station.

liverpool-street-elizabeth.history: Liverpool Street tube station opened on 12 July 1875 as part of the Metropolitan Railway's eastward extension from Moorgate, a year after the mainline terminus opened nearby. The Central line arrived in 1912, and the station gradually became one of the most heavily used interchanges in the City of London. Liverpool Street mainline handles services to East Anglia, Stansted Airport, and Hertfordshire, generating a consistent flow between rail and Underground. The Elizabeth line's arrival in 2022 significantly increased the station's scale and importance, connecting it directly west to the West End and Heathrow and east to Canary Wharf.
funFacts: Between 1938 and 1940, Liverpool Street mainline station was the arrival point for nearly 10,000 Jewish children brought to Britain from Nazi-occupied Europe on the Kindertransport. A memorial at the station, unveiled in 2006, commemorates them.

wood-lane-original.history: The original Wood Lane station opened in 1908 as the western terminus of the Central London Railway (precursor to the Central line), built primarily to serve the Franco-British Exhibition held nearby that year. It continued as a terminus until White City station opened in 1947, at which point Wood Lane closed and was replaced by the new station a short distance away.

wood-lane.history: Wood Lane opened on 27 October 2008 as part of the redevelopment of the former BBC Television Centre site, served from the outset by the Hammersmith & City line. From December 2009 Circle line trains also began calling here as part of the wider rerouting that ended the Circle's century-long closed-loop operation - the station's stop pattern changed without any new track being laid. It stands close to the original Wood Lane station that served the 1908 Franco-British Exhibition before closing in 1947. The BBC occupied Television Centre on the adjacent site from 1960 until 2013, when it sold the building for residential and commercial conversion - the curved white drum structure remains a recognisable west London landmark.

shepherd-bush-met.history: The original Shepherd's Bush station, opened on 13 June 1864 as part of the Metropolitan Railway's Hammersmith branch. Closed in 1914 when it was replaced by two new stations: Shepherd's Bush Market to the north and Goldhawk Road to the south.

shepherd-bush-market.history: The station opened on 1 April 1914 as Shepherd's Bush, replacing an earlier Metropolitan Railway station of the same name nearby. It was renamed Shepherd's Bush Market in 2008 when the new Shepherd's Bush Overground station opened a short distance away. The station was built to serve Shepherd's Bush Market - an open-air street market established in 1914 on a site previously occupied by the Central London Railway's electricity generating station. The market, still operating today, specialises in food, textiles, and household goods and is one of the few surviving traditional street markets in inner west London.

goldhawk-road.history: Goldhawk Road opened on 1 April 1914, built as the replacement for the original Shepherd's Bush Metropolitan Railway station and positioned closer to Hammersmith. The station serves the border between Shepherd's Bush and Chiswick, close to Ravenscourt Park to the south. Its name refers to Goldhawk Road itself, which follows a route through west London that predates the railway by several centuries and is thought to take its name from a family who owned land here in medieval times.

bethnal-green.history: Bethnal Green opened on 4 December 1946 as part of the Central line's eastern extension, though its tunnels had been substantially complete since 1939 when war halted construction. The unfinished station served throughout the Blitz as one of London's largest public air-raid shelters. On 3 March 1943 a crowd surging down the entrance steps after a nearby anti-aircraft battery fired caused a catastrophic crush, killing 173 people - the largest loss of civilian life in a single incident in Underground history. The disaster was suppressed under wartime censorship and not acknowledged until after the war. A central handrail - absent on the night - was fitted to the stairway before the station opened to passengers in 1946.
funFacts: The tunnels below Bethnal Green and nearby stations on the incomplete Central line eastern extension were requisitioned during the war as factories manufacturing aircraft components - sections of unfinished Underground tunnel converted into covered production lines while the rest of the structure served as shelter.

A memorial to the 173 victims was unveiled near the station entrance in 2017, more than 70 years after the event. The long delay was partly a consequence of wartime censorship having suppressed the story so effectively that many local people grew up unaware of the full scale of what had happened.

mile-end-central.history: Originally opened on 2 June 1902 by the Whitechapel and Bow Railway as a sub-surface station. Rebuilt and expanded in 1946 to accommodate the Central line's eastern extension, making it the only Underground station offering cross-platform interchange between deep-level tube and sub-surface trains.

mile-end.history: Originally opened on 2 June 1902 by the Whitechapel and Bow Railway as a sub-surface station. Rebuilt and expanded in 1946 to accommodate the Central line's eastern extension, making it the only Underground station offering cross-platform interchange between deep-level tube and sub-surface trains.

leyton.history: Originally opened in 1856 as Low Leyton, a mainline railway station built by the Eastern Counties Railway. Renamed Leyton in 1868. Transferred to London Underground and first served by the Central line on 5 May 1947 as part of the eastern extension.

leytonstone.history: Originally opened in 1856 as a mainline railway station by the Eastern Counties Railway. First served by the Central line on 5 May 1947, initially as a temporary terminus before the line extended further. The station is decorated with mosaics celebrating Alfred Hitchcock, who was born in Leytonstone in 1899.

marylebone.history: Opened on 27 March 1907 as 'Great Central' to serve the Great Central Railway terminus. Renamed Marylebone on 15 April 1917. One of the original Bakerloo line extension stations north of Baker Street.

edgware-road-bakerloo.history: Opened on 15 June 1907 as part of the Bakerloo line's northward extension from Baker Street. An entirely separate station from the Circle, District and Hammersmith & City line Edgware Road station nearby.

paddington-bakerloo.history: Opened on 1 December 1913 as part of the Bakerloo line's extension toward Queen's Park. An entirely separate deep-level station from the sub-surface Circle, District and Hammersmith & City line platforms.

warwick-avenue.history: Opened on 31 January 1915 as part of the Bakerloo line's extension to Queen's Park. Located close to Little Venice, the junction of the Grand Union and Regent's canals. Warwick Avenue was one of the earliest London Underground stations to be built with an entirely below-ground booking hall, accessed by modest street-level entrances rather than the dark ox-blood faience surface buildings designed by Stanley Heaps at the neighbouring stations of Maida Vale and Kilburn Park.

maida-vale.history: Opened on 6 June 1915, five months after the initial extension to Queen's Park, Maida Vale was the last of the four stations on the Bakerloo line's north-western extension to come into service. By the time it opened, Britain was a year into the First World War and the manpower shortage was acute: when the doors first opened the entire staff - ticket clerks, gatemen and lift operators - were women, making Maida Vale the first London Underground station to be run entirely by female personnel. The street-level building was designed by Stanley Heaps, the Underground Group's chief architect, in a style consciously continuing the work of his predecessor Leslie Green: a steel-framed two-storey block clad in the same distinctive ox-blood Burmantofts faience that defines stations like Russell Square and Holloway Road, with semi-circular upper windows engineered to allow future commercial overbuilding above the booking hall. Inside, the platforms retain their original biscuit-and-green tile panels with the station name worked into the mosaic in repeating cartouches - one of the most complete surviving Heaps-era schemes anywhere on the network. The station, which is served only by lifts from the deep-level tube platforms, was Grade II listed in 1986 for its architectural significance and is regarded as one of the finest preserved examples of pre-1920s Underground station design.
funFacts: The 'Maida' in Maida Vale commemorates an obscure Napoleonic-era battlefield in southern Italy. The district takes its name from the Hero of Maida pub, opened on Edgware Road around 1810 to honour Major-General Sir John Stuart's victory over the French at the Battle of Maida in Calabria on 4 July 1806 - making this one of very few Underground stations whose name ultimately derives from a small engagement fought more than a thousand miles from London.

Maida Vale has never had escalators. Like its Bakerloo extension neighbours it was built with passenger lifts only, descending from the booking hall directly to the deep-level platforms, and that arrangement has never been altered - the original 1915 lift shafts remain in daily use, though the cars and machinery within them have been replaced several times.

kilburn-park.history: Opened on 31 January 1915 as the temporary terminus of the Bakerloo line's extension from Paddington. Services were extended to Queen's Park just eleven days later on 11 February 1915. The station was designed by Stanley Heaps, who continued the dark ox-blood Burmantofts faience house style that Leslie Green had established for the deep-tube stations a decade earlier; the platforms still retain Heaps's biscuit-and-green tile scheme with the station name worked into repeating ornamental cartouches. The station was Grade II listed in 1986 for its architectural significance.

queens-park.history: Originally opened on 2 June 1879 by the London and North Western Railway. Bakerloo line services began on 11 February 1915, marking the transition from deep-level tube to overground running. Around a third of Bakerloo trains terminate here.

kensal-green.history: Opened on 1 October 1916 on the Watford DC line, with Bakerloo services running through from Queen's Park to Willesden Junction since May 1915. The station takes its name from Kensal Green Cemetery, one of the 'Magnificent Seven' Victorian garden cemeteries established to relieve the pressure on London's overcrowded parish churchyards.

willesden-junction.history: Bakerloo line services began running through Willesden Junction on 10 May 1915, operating over the London and North Western Railway's Watford DC line tracks. The station remains a major interchange on the London Overground network, connecting the Bakerloo line with several routes serving north-west London.

harlesden.history: The station opened on 15 June 1912 on the Watford DC line. Bakerloo line services began on 16 April 1917 when the line was extended northward from Willesden Junction to Watford Junction.

stonebridge-park.history: Opened on 1 August 1917 on the Watford DC line. Home to the Bakerloo line's main depot, which opened in 1979.

wembley-central.history: The station's history predates the Underground by more than two decades: it opened on 20 July 1842 as plain 'Sudbury', built by the London and Birmingham Railway on its newly extended main line out of Euston. It was renamed Sudbury & Wembley in 1882 as the growing hamlet next door began to assert itself, then Wembley for Sudbury on 1 November 1910 - by which point Wembley was the better-known of the two place names. Bakerloo line services began on 16 April 1917 when London Electric Railways extended onto the London and North Western Railway's Watford DC line. The final rename to plain Wembley Central came on 5 July 1948, timed for the London Olympic Games at Wembley Stadium opening just three weeks later. Today the station is a major Bakerloo and London Overground interchange, handling significant additional passenger flows on event days at Wembley Stadium and the OVO Arena.
funFacts: The station's first incarnation - as plain 'Sudbury' on the London and Birmingham Railway - opened on 20 July 1842, twenty-one years before the world's first underground railway ran between Paddington and Farringdon in January 1863. Few Underground stations have older railway antecedents, and even fewer have survived through being renamed three separate times along the way.

Despite its name, Wembley Central is not the closest tube station to Wembley Stadium - that distinction belongs to Wembley Park on the Metropolitan and Jubilee lines, half a mile to the north along Olympic Way. Wembley Central handles overflow crowds on big-event days, with the parallel London Overground service providing rapid dispersal to north-west and central London.

north-wembley.history: First served by the Bakerloo line on 16 April 1917 as part of the extension to Watford Junction.

south-kenton.history: Opened on 3 July 1933, the last of the above-ground Bakerloo stations to open, serving the developing residential suburbs of northwest London.

kenton.history: First served by the Bakerloo line on 16 April 1917. Located close to Northwick Park on the Metropolitan line.

harrow-wealdstone.history: Originally opened on 1 May 1897 by the London and North Western Railway. Bakerloo line services began on 16 April 1917 as the northern terminus of the extension to Watford Junction. The station was the site of a serious crash in 1952 in which 112 people were killed.

west-brompton.history: Opened on 12 April 1869 by the District Railway as the terminus of its extension from Gloucester Road, before Earl's Court station opened in 1871. The station building was designed by engineer Sir John Fowler in a Classical style, featuring white Suffolk brickwork and a slate pavilion roof. It is a Grade II listed building. Today it is an interchange with the London Overground Mildmay line and National Rail services on the West London Line.

fulham-broadway.history: Opened on 1 March 1880 as Walham Green, as part of the District Railway extension from West Brompton to Putney Bridge. Renamed Fulham Broadway on 2 March 1952. The entrance building was rebuilt in 1910 to a design by Harry W. Ford, architect to the District Railway, in an ornate Edwardian Baroque style clad in brown faience. The original entrance building and trainshed are Grade II listed. The station was significantly upgraded in the early 2000s when the Fulham Broadway Shopping Centre was built over it. Nearest station to Chelsea FC's Stamford Bridge.

parsons-green.history: Opened on 1 March 1880 as part of the District Railway extension from West Brompton to Putney Bridge. Named after the historic green at the heart of the surrounding village. The station serves a predominantly residential area of Fulham known for its Victorian and Edwardian terraces.

putney-bridge.history: Opened on 1 March 1880 as Putney Bridge & Fulham, serving as the line's terminus until 1889 when the District Railway extended south to Wimbledon via Fulham Railway Bridge across the Thames. Despite its name, the station is on the Fulham (north) side of the river. A World War II pillbox remains visible at the southern end of the southbound platform. Renamed to its current name in 1932. Nearest station to Fulham FC's Craven Cottage.

east-putney.history: Opened on 3 June 1889 as part of the District Railway extension from Putney Bridge to Wimbledon, built over London and South Western Railway tracks. The station has a distinctive Y-shaped island platform arrangement reflecting its junction with the LSWR line. Regular mainline services through the station ended in 1941, and the line remained in British Rail ownership until 1994, when it was transferred to London Underground for a nominal £1.

southfields.history: Opened on 3 June 1889 on the District Railway extension from Putney Bridge to Wimbledon. The station is the principal access point for the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club during the Wimbledon Championships, with passenger numbers more than tripling during the fortnight. Southfields and Wimbledon Park stations share an almost identical original Victorian design, though mirrored. The line remained in British Rail ownership until 1994.

wimbledon-park.history: Opened on 3 June 1889 on the District Railway extension from Putney Bridge to Wimbledon. Originally called Wimbledon Park (Southfields), it was renamed in 1950. The station shares its original Victorian design with Southfields, though the two are mirror images of each other. The line remained in British Rail ownership until 1994. Step-free access was added in September 2021.

wimbledon.history: The District Railway opened its extension to Wimbledon on 3 June 1889, using London and South Western Railway tracks from East Putney. The station was rebuilt on its current site for the opening of this service. Steam services on the branch were replaced by electric trains on 27 August 1905 - the last part of the District line to be electrified. The current Portland stone entrance building was constructed by the Southern Railway in the late 1920s. Today Wimbledon is an interchange with South Western Railway, Tramlink, and is globally associated with the Wimbledon Tennis Championships.

ravenscourt-park.history: The line through the station was opened on 1 January 1869 by the London and South Western Railway, but the station itself opened as Shaftesbury Road on 1 April 1873. District Railway services began on 1 June 1877 when the DR extended from Hammersmith to connect to the L&SWR tracks here. Renamed Ravenscourt Park in 1888. The station has four tracks with two island platforms - the inner pair for the Piccadilly line, which runs through without stopping. Named after the nearby 32-acre park, which incorporates the former gardens of Ravenscourt House, built in 1650.

stamford-brook.history: The line through the station was opened on 1 January 1869 by the London and South Western Railway, with District Railway services running through from 1 June 1877, but the station itself did not open until 1 February 1912. It holds a small piece of London Underground history: on 5 January 1964 it became the first station on the network to install an automatic ticket barrier, a device which optically scanned a printed ternary code on each ticket. Named after the Stamford Brook, a now largely underground tributary of the Thames.

turnham-green.history: Opened on 1 January 1869 by the London and South Western Railway on its new branch to Richmond. District Railway services began on 1 June 1877. The station became a junction on 1 July 1879 when the District opened its Ealing Broadway branch. The Piccadilly line was extended through the station on 4 July 1932, though Piccadilly trains stop here only during early mornings and late evenings. A 1913 Central London Railway proposal to extend to Richmond would have placed a deep-level station here - but the plan was abandoned after World War I.

gunnersbury.history: Opened as Brentford Road on 1 January 1869 by the London and South Western Railway. District Railway services began on 1 June 1877 when the DR began running over L&SWR tracks to Richmond. Renamed Gunnersbury in 1871. The Richmond branch tracks through the station were electrified on 1 August 1905, funded by the District Railway. Today it is also served by the London Overground Mildmay line. Adjacent to Gunnersbury Park, a 200-acre public park containing two Grade II-listed Georgian mansions.

kew-gardens.history: Opened on 1 January 1869 by the London and South Western Railway, with District Railway services beginning on 1 June 1877. Named after the Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew - one of the world's most important centres for plant science and conservation, designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2003. The station is also served by the London Overground Mildmay line.

richmond.history: District Railway services began running to Richmond on 1 June 1877, using London and South Western Railway tracks from Hammersmith. The current station building dates from 1937, designed by Southern Railway chief architect James Robb Scott in Art Deco style, featuring a Portland stone facade with a square clock. Richmond is today a major interchange with South Western Railway and the London Overground Mildmay line. The District is the only Underground line to cross the River Thames on a bridge, doing so on both the Wimbledon and Richmond branches.

chiswick-park.history: Opened on 1 July 1879 by the Metropolitan District Railway on its extension from Turnham Green to Ealing Broadway, originally named Acton Green. Renamed Chiswick Park and Acton Green in 1887, and given its current name on 1 March 1910. Rebuilt between 1931 and 1932 in preparation for the Piccadilly line western extension. The station is Grade II listed.

acton-town.history: Opened on 1 July 1879 as Mill Hill Park by the District Railway on its extension from Turnham Green to Ealing Broadway. Renamed Acton Town on 1 March 1910. The current station building was designed by Charles Holden in 1931–32 in his characteristic modernist style - a tall block-like ticket hall of brick and reinforced concrete rising above a low single-storey podium. The Piccadilly line was extended here on 4 July 1932. The station is Grade II listed and is an important junction - west of Acton Town, District trains serve Ealing Broadway and Piccadilly trains serve Heathrow and Uxbridge.

ealing-common.history: Opened on 1 July 1879 by the Metropolitan District Railway as part of the extension from Turnham Green to Ealing Broadway. The station is the gateway to the District line's Ealing Common Depot to the north. The Piccadilly line was extended through the station on 4 July 1932, and from that date District line services west of Ealing Common to South Harrow and Uxbridge were taken over by the Piccadilly line.

ealing-common-piccadilly.history: Opened on 1 July 1879 by the Metropolitan District Railway as part of the extension from Turnham Green to Ealing Broadway. The station is the gateway to the District line's Ealing Common Depot to the north. The Piccadilly line was extended through the station on 4 July 1932, and from that date District line services west of Ealing Common to South Harrow and Uxbridge were taken over by the Piccadilly line.

ealing-broadway.history: Opened on 1 July 1879 as the western terminus of the Metropolitan District Railway's extension from Turnham Green. The station is today a major interchange with the Elizabeth line and Great Western Railway services. The District line platforms are north of the Central line and GWR platforms. Ealing Broadway is one of the westernmost points of the District line.

aldgate-east.history: Opened on 6 October 1884 as part of the eastern extension completing the Circle line, jointly operated by the Metropolitan and District railways. The original station was situated approximately 150 metres west of the current site. As part of the 1935–40 New Works Programme, over 900 workmen simultaneously lowered the entire track by more than seven feet in a single night using overhead hooks, and the station was rebuilt and resited eastward, opening on 31 October 1938. The platform walls feature distinctive relief tiles depicting London landmarks, designed by Harold Stabler. The nearby St Mary's station closed when the rebuilt Aldgate East effectively replaced it.

stepney-green.history: Opened on 23 June 1902 by the Whitechapel and Bow Railway - a joint venture between the District Railway and the London, Tilbury and Southend Railway - on a new route connecting Whitechapel with the LT&SR at Bow. The station was not ready for the first day of service (2 June 1902) and opened with temporary buildings. The permanent structures were designed by the line's chief engineer, Cuthbert Arthur Brereton. Electric services replaced steam trains on 20 August 1905. Metropolitan line services began in 1936.

bow-road.history: Opened on 11 June 1902 by the Whitechapel and Bow Railway, nine days after the line opened, as the station was not ready on the first day of service. The permanent structures were designed by the line's chief engineer, Cuthbert Arthur Brereton. Electric services replaced steam trains on 20 August 1905. The adjacent Wellington Buildings flats, accessed from Wellington Street, were built to house the 286 people displaced when the railway was constructed. Metropolitan line service commenced in 1936.

bromley-by-bow.history: The Whitechapel and Bow Railway opened on 2 June 1902, connecting the District Railway at Whitechapel to the London, Tilbury and Southend Railway at Bromley-by-Bow, where it joined the LT&SR's existing tracks. The station itself had been operating as a mainline station since 1869 under various names. District Railway electric service began on 30 September 1905. Metropolitan line service commenced in 1936. The fictional Walford East station in the BBC soap opera EastEnders takes the place of Bromley-by-Bow on the tube map.

plaistow.history: Opened on 31 March 1858 by the London, Tilbury and Southend Railway on its new direct route from Fenchurch Street to Barking, one of the original three stations on the line. District Railway services began on 2 June 1902 following the opening of the Whitechapel and Bow Railway junction. The two-storey booking hall dates from 1882–83 and is locally listed for its historic interest. Surviving benches and canopy supports still bear the LTSR initials. Metropolitan line service commenced in 1936. Mainline services were withdrawn in 1962.

upton-park.history: Opened on 17 September 1877 by the London, Tilbury and Southend Railway as an infill station between Plaistow and East Ham - the first station on the LTSR to be built by a private property developer, designed to serve the Upton Park housing estate. District Railway services began on 2 June 1902. The large Edwardian station building dates from the 1902 rebuild for District Railway service. Metropolitan line service commenced in 1936. The station is close to the former Boleyn Ground, home of West Ham United until 2016.

east-ham.history: Opened on 31 March 1858 by the London, Tilbury and Southend Railway, one of the original three stations on the direct Fenchurch Street to Barking line. District Railway services began on 2 June 1902. Following electrification in 1905, the station briefly served as the eastern terminus of electric District services, with passengers transferring to steam trains for Upminster until full electrification reached Barking in 1908. The Edwardian brick station building spanning the tracks dates from the 1902 rebuild and is Grade II listed. Metropolitan line service commenced in 1936.

barking.history: District Railway services began running to Barking on 2 June 1902 following the opening of the Whitechapel and Bow Railway, initially using steam traction over LT&SR tracks. Full electric District services reached Barking on 1 April 1908. Barking is a major interchange with the London Overground Gospel Oak to Barking line and c2c National Rail services. The current station building was opened by Her Majesty the Queen in 1961 and is listed for its architecture - a modernist design notable for its protruding concrete roof requiring no pillars. The Hammersmith & City line terminates here.

white-city.architect: A.D. McGill and Kenneth J.H. Seymour
white-city.history: Opened on 23 November 1947, replacing the earlier Wood Lane station whose anti-clockwise loop layout had become operationally redundant. Construction began in 1938 and was scheduled to complete by 1940, but the Second World War delayed opening by seven years. The station sits in a deep brick-sided cutting and is a surface station - trains enter the tube tunnels immediately to the east. An unusual quirk is that trains run on the right-hand track through the station rather than the conventional left, a legacy of the old Wood Lane loop geometry. The station's design won an Award of Merit at the 1951 Festival of Britain. It is directly opposite the former BBC Television Centre and within walking distance of Westfield London.
funFacts: Trains run on the right-hand track through White City - the only such arrangement on the Central line, inherited from the old Wood Lane loop.
The station's design won an Award of Merit at the 1951 Festival of Britain, recognised with a commemorative Poole Pottery plaque.
Construction started in 1938 but World War II delayed the opening by seven years.

east-acton.history: Opened on 3 August 1920 as the only intermediate station on the Central London Railway's new extension to Ealing Broadway, running over Great Western Railway tracks. The line through East Acton was built by the GWR following parliamentary approval in 1911, with electrification delayed by the First World War. Until 1938, GWR steam freight trains shared the route alongside Central line passenger services, on two dedicated freight tracks north of the station. The eastbound platform was rebuilt between August 2021 and September 2022 after structural elements were found to require full replacement.
funFacts: When it opened in 1920, East Acton was the only intermediate stop on the entire Ealing Broadway extension.
GWR steam freight trains ran alongside Central line passengers through East Acton until 1938, on a pair of dedicated freight tracks.
The eastern platform rebuild in 2021–22 was originally planned to take five months but took over a year after unexpected structural issues were discovered.

north-acton.history: Opened on 5 November 1923, built and owned by the Great Western Railway to serve the Central London Railway's Ealing Broadway extension, which had been running through the site without a station since 1920. North Acton is a junction station where the West Ruislip branch diverges from the Ealing Broadway branch. A third platform was added in 1992 to improve operational flexibility, allowing shuttle services to be run independently on each branch.
funFacts: Central line trains had been running through North Acton for three years before the station finally opened in 1923.
North Acton was originally going to be called 'Victoria Road'.
A third platform was added in 1992 by converting the old freight line trackbed, allowing the Ealing Broadway and West Ruislip branches to operate independently if needed.

west-acton.history: Opened on 5 November 1923, built and owned by the Great Western Railway alongside North Acton to serve the Central London Railway's Ealing Broadway extension. The current station building replaced the original in the late 1930s as part of the London Passenger Transport Board's 1935–40 New Works Programme, designed by GWR chief architect Brian Lewis. It is the only station on the Ealing Broadway branch between North Acton and Ealing Broadway. West Acton is a Grade II listed building, the only station on the Central line's western branches completed fully to Lewis's original pre-war designs.
funFacts: West Acton is a Grade II listed building - the only station on the Central line's western extension completed fully to Brian Lewis's original 1930s designs.
Lewis emigrated to Australia after the war and became Professor of Architecture at the University of Melbourne, later known for the National University campus in Canberra.
GWR steam freight trains ran through West Acton until 1938, sharing the route with Underground passenger services.

south-ealing.history: Opened on 1 May 1883 by the District Railway as part of the extension from Acton Town towards Hounslow. Piccadilly line services began in 1935, two years after the rest of the Hounslow branch, due to the close proximity of the rebuilt Northfields station which caused operational complications. The station was substantially rebuilt in the early 1930s in preparation for the Piccadilly line extension.

northfields.history: Opened as Northfield (Ealing) Halt on 16 April 1908, the station was rebuilt in 1932 to a design by Charles Holden in preparation for Piccadilly line services, which began on 9 January 1933. The rebuilt station is a Grade II listed building, featuring Holden's characteristic tall block-like ticket hall with clerestory windows and a flat concrete roof. The station connects directly to Northfields depot, the main maintenance facility for Piccadilly line trains. District line services were withdrawn on 10 October 1964.
funFacts: Northfields depot immediately west of the station is the main overnight stabling and maintenance facility for the entire Piccadilly line fleet.
The westbound running track passes under the depot exit track in a cutting to avoid operational conflicts - an unusual piece of underground engineering visible from the platform.
Charles Holden's 1932 design was listed Grade II in 1994, recognised as an exemplar of modernist transport architecture.

boston-manor.history: Opened on 1 May 1883 by the Metropolitan District Railway as Boston Road, renamed Boston Manor on 11 December 1911. The station was rebuilt between 1932 and 1934 in a Modernist style in preparation for the Piccadilly line extension, which reached here on 13 March 1933 while building works were still ongoing - the new station building was not fully completed until 25 March 1934. District line services were withdrawn on 9 October 1964. The station sits close to Boston Manor House, a Jacobean mansion dating from 1622.

osterley.history: Opened on 25 March 1934, replacing the earlier Osterley & Spring Grove station located 300 metres to the east. Designed by Stanley Heaps following a preliminary plan by Charles Holden, in the modern European style used across the Piccadilly line extensions. The station features a distinctive brick tower topped with a concrete obelisk, possibly inspired by the De Telegraaf Building in Amsterdam which Holden visited on a study trip. Grade II listed since 1987. District line services were withdrawn on 9 October 1964.
funFacts: The concrete obelisk atop Osterley's brick tower was likely inspired by the De Telegraaf Building in Amsterdam, which Charles Holden visited during a European study tour.
The original Osterley & Spring Grove station building still survives 300 metres to the east, though it has been disused since 1934.
Osterley is the closest tube station to Sky's offices near Gillette Corner on the Great West Road.

hounslow-east.history: Opened on 2 May 1909 as Hounslow Town, replacing the original Hounslow Town station which had a complicated history involving a short-lived loop operation. Renamed Hounslow East on 1 December 1925. Piccadilly line services extended here on 13 March 1933. District line services were withdrawn on 9 October 1964. The station was subsequently rebuilt with a modern glass-enclosed ticket hall on the south side of the tracks.

hounslow-central.history: Opened on 1 April 1886 as Heston & Hounslow, replacing the original Hounslow Town station on the same day it closed. Renamed Hounslow Central on 1 December 1925. The route through the station was opened by the District Railway on 21 July 1884 as a single-track branch to Hounslow Barracks. Piccadilly line services extended here on 13 March 1933. District line services were withdrawn on 9 October 1964.

hounslow-west.history: Opened as Hounslow Barracks on 21 July 1884 by the Hounslow & Metropolitan Railway, named after the nearby Cavalry Barracks. Renamed Hounslow West on 1 December 1925. The station building was rebuilt to a design by Charles Holden in 1931 in preparation for the Piccadilly line extension, which arrived on 13 March 1933. District line services were fully withdrawn on 9 October 1964. In 1975 the platforms were relocated underground as part of the Heathrow extension, with a new link constructed to the existing Holden station building.
funFacts: Winston Churchill recalled travelling to Hounslow Barracks two or three times a week in the 1890s while living at his mother's house in Knightsbridge.
Hounslow West is the only London Underground station where step-free access is available only for manual wheelchair users, via a stairlift rather than a lift.
When the Heathrow extension opened in 1977, Hounslow West lost its airport bus services - they relocated to the new Hatton Cross station instead.

hatton-cross.history: Opened on 19 July 1975 as the first phase of the Piccadilly line's extension from Hounslow West towards Heathrow Airport, serving as the terminus for two years until Heathrow Central opened on 16 December 1977. Built using the cut-and-cover method, the station features platform tiling incorporating the British Airways Speedbird logo. On its opening day, Hatton Cross brought the total number of active London Underground stations to 279 - the highest figure ever recorded.
funFacts: When Hatton Cross opened in 1975 it brought the total number of active tube stations to 279 - the all-time record, which has since fallen to 272.
The platform columns feature the British Airways Speedbird logo in their tiling, a nod to the station's proximity to Heathrow.
Just east of the station, the Piccadilly line briefly surfaces to cross the River Crane before descending back underground towards Hounslow West.

heathrow-terminals-2-3.history: Opened as Heathrow Central by Queen Elizabeth II on 16 December 1977, becoming the first airport in the world to be directly connected to an underground railway. Renamed Heathrow Central Terminals 1, 2, 3 in 1983 ahead of the Terminal 4 opening, then Heathrow Terminals 1, 2, 3 in 1986, and finally Heathrow Terminals 2 & 3 in 2016 following the demolition of Terminal 1. The platforms feature an abstract mural of Concorde in vitreous enamel by artist Tom Eckersley.
funFacts: When it opened in 1977, Heathrow Central was the first airport in the world to be directly served by an underground railway.
The platforms feature a vitreous enamel mural of Concorde by artist Tom Eckersley - fitting, as Concorde was still in regular service from Heathrow at the time.
The station has been renamed four times: Heathrow Central (1977), Heathrow Central Terminals 1,2,3 (1983), Heathrow Terminals 1,2,3 (1986), and Heathrow Terminals 2 & 3 (2016).

heathrow-terminal-5.history: Opened on 27 March 2008 as the western terminus of the Piccadilly line's Heathrow branch, serving the new British Airways Terminal 5. It was the first stretch of new Underground railway built since the Jubilee line extension opened in 1999. Alternate Piccadilly line trains serve Terminal 5 directly from Terminals 2 & 3, while the other half continue via the Terminal 4 loop.
funFacts: Heathrow Terminal 5 was the first new Underground station to open since the Jubilee line extension in 1999 - a nine-year gap.
Only alternate Piccadilly line trains serve Terminal 5; the others take the Terminal 4 loop, so passengers need to check the destination board before boarding.
The station is entirely within the Heathrow Airport security perimeter, making it unique among London Underground stations.

north-ealing.history: Opened on 23 June 1903 by the District Railway as part of its extension from Ealing Common towards South Harrow. The Piccadilly line took over from the District on 4 July 1932. North Ealing was the only station on the South Harrow branch not rebuilt in the 1930s Holden style for the Piccadilly line extension, retaining its original character.

park-royal.history: The original station, Park Royal & Twyford Abbey, opened on 23 June 1903 by the District Railway. The current station opened on 6 July 1931, replacing the original a day earlier, built in preparation for the Piccadilly line extension. The Piccadilly took over from the District on 4 July 1932. The station sits close to the A40 Western Avenue and the Piccadilly line crosses over the Central line tracks just to the north.

alperton.history: Opened on 28 June 1903 by the District Railway as Perivale Alperton, renamed Alperton on 7 October 1910. The Piccadilly line took over from the District on 4 July 1932. The station was rebuilt in the Charles Holden style in preparation for the Piccadilly extension, featuring the characteristic tall block-like ticket hall with clerestory windows.

sudbury-town.history: Opened on 28 June 1903 by the District Railway. The current station building was designed by Charles Holden and opened in 1931 in preparation for the Piccadilly line takeover on 4 July 1932. Sudbury Town was the first of Holden's new stations to open and established the template - a tall rectangular brick box with a flat concrete slab roof and panels of vertical glazing - that was reproduced across many subsequent stations. It is a Grade II* listed building.
funFacts: Sudbury Town was the prototype for Charles Holden's 1930s Underground station design - its 'brick box with a concrete lid' template was reproduced at Acton Town, Sudbury Hill, Alperton and many others.
Some original signage at Sudbury Town uses Johnston Delf Smith, a wedge-serif variation of the standard Johnston typeface developed by Holden specifically for these stations.
The station is Grade II* listed - a higher designation than Grade II, indicating it is particularly important.

sudbury-hill.history: Opened on 28 June 1903 by the District Railway. Rebuilt in the early 1930s to Charles Holden's modern European style in preparation for the Piccadilly line extension, which took over from the District on 4 July 1932. Grade II listed since 1994.

south-harrow.history: Opened on 28 June 1903 by the District Railway as the terminus of its new extension from Park Royal & Twyford Abbey. Extended north to Rayners Lane on 1 March 1910. The Piccadilly line took over from the District on 4 July 1932, with an isolated District service continuing north to Uxbridge until 23 October 1933 when the Piccadilly took over that section too.

rayners-lane.history: Opened as Rayners Lane Halt on 26 May 1906 by the Metropolitan Railway, named after local farmer Daniel Rayner. District line services began on 1 March 1910 when the extension from South Harrow connected here. The Piccadilly line took over District services on 23 October 1933. The current station was rebuilt to a design by Charles Holden and Reginald Uren, opening on 8 August 1938, featuring a large cube-shaped brick and glass ticket hall. It is Grade II listed. Rayners Lane is where the Piccadilly and Metropolitan line tracks join for westbound services to Uxbridge.
funFacts: Rayners Lane was nicknamed 'Pneumonia Junction' due to its exposed location on an embankment, swept by wind from all directions.
The station's passenger numbers exploded from 22,000 per year in 1930 to 4 million by 1937, driven by rapid suburban development in the area.
The Metropolitan and Piccadilly lines share track west of Rayners Lane to Uxbridge but use entirely separate tracks east of it towards central London.

eastcote.history: Opened as Eastcote Halt on 26 May 1906 by the Metropolitan Railway. District line services began 1 March 1910. The Piccadilly line took over District services on 23 October 1933. The station was rebuilt between 1937 and 1939 to a Charles Holden design featuring the large cube-shaped brick and glass ticket hall. Both the station buildings and platforms are Grade II listed.

ruislip-manor.history: Opened as Ruislip Manor Halt on 5 August 1912 by the Metropolitan Railway, though District line trains had been running through the site since 1910. The station was a response to suburban development in the area. District line services were replaced by the Piccadilly line on 23 October 1933.

ruislip.history: Opened on 4 July 1904 as the only intermediate station on the original Harrow and Uxbridge Railway between Harrow-on-the-Hill and Uxbridge. District line services began 1 March 1910. The Piccadilly line took over District services on 23 October 1933. Ruislip is the oldest station on the Uxbridge branch.

ickenham.history: Opened as Ickenham Halt on 25 September 1905 following lobbying by the Ickenham Parish Council - the Metropolitan Railway had been reluctant to open a station due to perceived lack of revenue. Local villagers sold flowers and served teas to passengers from the halt. District line services began 1 March 1910. The Piccadilly line took over District services on 23 October 1933. The halt was rebuilt as a proper station in 1970–71.
funFacts: Ickenham station only exists because the local Parish Council lobbied the Metropolitan Railway - the company initially refused to open a halt due to low expected revenue.
When it first opened, local villagers sold flowers from their gardens and served teas to passengers arriving from London.
The platform shelters dating from 1905 survived until 1970–71 when the station was finally rebuilt into its current form.

hillingdon.history: Opened on 10 December 1923, the last of the intermediate stations to open on the Uxbridge branch. District line trains had been running through the site since 1910 without stopping. The Piccadilly line took over District services on 23 October 1933.

uxbridge.history: The Harrow and Uxbridge Railway first opened a station at Uxbridge on 4 July 1904 on Belmont Road, worked from the outset by the Metropolitan Railway. District line services began 1 March 1910. The Piccadilly line took over District services on 23 October 1933. The current station building, designed by Adams, Holden & Pearson, opened on 4 December 1938 when the line was realigned closer to the town centre.
funFacts: The original 1904 Uxbridge station was on Belmont Road, north of the current station - the line was realigned and a new station built in 1938 to serve the town centre better.
Uxbridge is the western terminus of both the Metropolitan and Piccadilly lines - the two longest lines on the Underground network.
At 15.5 miles west of Charing Cross, Uxbridge is one of the most geographically remote stations on the entire Underground network.

west-harrow.history: Opened on 17 November 1913 by the Metropolitan Railway, though the line through the site had been running since 4 July 1904. West Harrow is the only station on the Uxbridge branch of the Metropolitan line served exclusively by the Metropolitan - neither the Piccadilly nor any other line calls here.

north-harrow.history: Opened on 22 March 1915 by the Metropolitan Railway, though the line through the site had been running since 25 May 1885. The station was rebuilt in 1930 to designs by Charles Clark as part of the Metropolitan Railway's modernisation programme.

pinner.history: Opened on 25 May 1885, it remained the terminus of the Metropolitan Railway until 1 September 1887 when the line was extended to Rickmansworth. The long single-storey station building on the southbound platform is the original 1885 structure, resembling the stations at Rickmansworth, Chorleywood and Chalfont & Latimer. In 1915 the Metro-land project was conceived here, promoting suburban living in rural Middlesex.
funFacts: Pinner was the terminus of the Metropolitan Railway for two years from 1885 to 1887.
Houses built near Pinner during the Metro-land expansion had to be worth at least £1,000 - higher than the £750 minimum required at Harrow-on-the-Hill, reflecting Pinner's more affluent character.
The original 1885 station building still stands on the southbound platform, one of the oldest surviving Metropolitan Railway structures.

northwood-hills.history: Opened on 13 November 1933, infilling a gap between Pinner (1885) and Northwood (1887) to serve new residential development in the area. The name was chosen by competition - the winning entry was suggested by a woman from North Harrow. Despite its name, the area is lower in elevation than Northwood.
funFacts: The station's name was chosen by public competition - won by a woman from North Harrow.
Despite being called Northwood Hills, the station is actually lower in elevation than nearby Northwood.
Its neighbouring stations - Pinner (1885) and Northwood (1887) - both opened nearly 50 years before Northwood Hills.

northwood.history: Opened on 1 September 1887 on the Metropolitan Railway's extension from Pinner to Rickmansworth. The station was rebuilt in the 1960s so that its platforms are on the slow lines rather than the fast lines - fast services do not stop here. The station serves as the key connection for the town of Northwood to London, as the line provides the sole continuous rail link between the two.

moor-park.history: Opened on 9 May 1910 as Sandy Lodge, named after the nearby Sandy Lodge Golf Course. Renamed Moor Park & Sandy Lodge in 1923. Moor Park is the junction station where the Watford branch diverges from the Amersham main line. The station has four platforms - two for the Amersham/Chesham services and two for the Watford branch.
funFacts: Moor Park was originally called Sandy Lodge after the golf course next to it - hardly the most inspiring name for a tube station.
Moor Park is one of the few stations outside Greater London served by the Underground, sitting in the Three Rivers district of Hertfordshire.
The station sits at the junction of the Amersham and Watford branches - trains split here for their different destinations.

rickmansworth.history: Opened on 1 September 1887 on the Metropolitan Railway's extension from Pinner. Until 1961, Rickmansworth was the changeover point where steam locomotives were exchanged for electric ones on trains from Aylesbury to London, a practice that lasted until electrification was extended north to Amersham. Rickmansworth is still the operational headquarters of the northern section of the Metropolitan line, controlling signals from Northwood to Watford and Chorleywood.
funFacts: Until 1961, passengers on trains from Aylesbury would sit at Rickmansworth for several minutes while steam locomotives were swapped for electric ones - a daily ritual for 28 years.
Rickmansworth is still the driver changeover point on the Metropolitan line - most northbound trains pause here for a few minutes while drivers swap.

Douglas Adams - author of The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy - famously wrote that 'far out in the uncharted backwaters of the unfashionable end of the Western Spiral arm of the Galaxy lies a small unregarded yellow sun,' but it was in Rickmansworth that Arthur Dent had his epiphany about life.

chorleywood.history: Opened in July 1889 as Chorley Wood, renamed Chorley Wood & Chenies in November 1915, then reverting to Chorley Wood in 1934 before adopting the present spelling in 1965. Also served by Chiltern Railways. The station was formerly served by steam-hauled Metropolitan line trains from Aylesbury, with a changeover to electric locomotive at Rickmansworth until 1961 electrification.

chalfont-latimer.history: Opened as Chalfont Road on 8 July 1889, renamed Chalfont & Latimer on 1 November 1915. The station is the junction between the main line to Amersham and the single-track branch to Chesham. It is also served by Chiltern Railways services to Aylesbury. The station has three platforms - two for the main line and one bay platform for the Chesham shuttle.
funFacts: Chalfont & Latimer is the junction for the Chesham branch - one of the most rural and remote-feeling sections of the entire Underground network.
The gap between Chesham and Chalfont & Latimer is the longest between adjacent stations on the entire London Underground at 3.89 miles.
The station serves three Chalfonts: Chalfont St Giles, Chalfont St Peter and Little Chalfont, as well as the village of Latimer.

amersham.history: Opened on 1 September 1892 as part of the Metropolitan Railway's extension from Chalfont Road to Aylesbury. Amersham is the current terminus of the Metropolitan line and one of the most remote stations on the Underground network at 25 miles from central London. The area around the station became known as Amersham on the Hill or New Town, distinct from the historic Old Amersham about a mile away. Metropolitan line services north of Amersham to Aylesbury were withdrawn in 1961 when the line was electrified and services handed to British Railways.
funFacts: Amersham is the joint-farthest station from central London on the Underground, at 25 miles from Charing Cross.
The station sits in New Amersham - a separate settlement that grew up around the railway a mile from the historic Old Town, which predates the railway by centuries.
Until 1961, through trains ran from Amersham to Aylesbury and beyond hauled by steam locomotives - making it one of the last steam-worked sections of the London Underground.

chesham.history: Opened on 8 July 1889 as the original northern terminus of the Metropolitan Railway from Baker Street. When the main line was extended through Chalfont Road to Amersham in 1892, Chesham became a branch line terminus. Today it is served by a shuttle from Chalfont & Latimer, except for a few peak-hour through trains. Chesham is both the northernmost and westernmost station on the London Underground, and at 25 miles from Charing Cross, jointly the farthest from central London.
funFacts: Chesham is both the northernmost and westernmost station on the entire London Underground network.
At 3.89 miles, the gap between Chesham and Chalfont & Latimer is the longest between any two adjacent stations on the Underground.
The station celebrated its 125th anniversary in August 2014 with a steam special using Metropolitan Railway locomotive No.1, the network's first steam locomotive.

croxley.history: Opened on 2 November 1925 as Croxley Green on the Metropolitan Railway's new Watford branch. Renamed Croxley in 1949 to avoid confusion with a nearby British Rail station also called Croxley Green. The station is the only intermediate stop on the Watford branch. The long-proposed Croxley Rail Link, which would have diverted the branch to Watford Junction and closed this station, was finally cancelled in 2018 after years of funding disputes.

watford-met.history: Opened on 2 November 1925 as the terminus of the Metropolitan Railway's Watford branch. Designed by Metropolitan Railway architect Charles Walter Clark in an Arts and Crafts vernacular style with red brick, clay-tiled hipped roofs and tall chimney stacks. Despite promotional posters depicting Watford High Street, the station sits over a mile from the town centre in the Cassiobury area, which hampered passenger numbers. The cancelled Croxley Rail Link would have closed this station and diverted the line to Watford Junction.
funFacts: The Metropolitan Railway's 1925 promotional poster 'By Metro to Watford' depicted the busy High Street market - yet the station was built over a mile away from it.
Watford tube station is known locally as 'Watford Met' to distinguish it from the much busier Watford Junction and Watford High Street stations nearby.
