The Croxley Rail Link - which would have closed this station - was cancelled in 2018 after 16 years of planning and funding disputes.

clapham-north.architect: T. P. Figgis (1900); Charles Holden (1924 rebuild)
clapham-north.history: Opened on 3 June 1900 as Clapham Road, the southern terminus of the City & South London Railway's extension from Stockwell. The original building by T. P. Figgis was rebuilt in 1923–24 by Charles Holden - his first major Underground commission - replacing the facade with distinctive biscuit-cream faience slabs and installing escalators. Renamed Clapham North on 13 September 1926 when the line was extended to Morden. One of only two remaining Underground stations with a narrow island platform serving both directions, the other being the next stop, Clapham Common. It is one of eight stations with a deep-level wartime air raid shelter beneath it.
funFacts: Originally named Clapham Road - it was renamed Clapham North only when Morden opened to distinguish it from stations further south
One of only two stations left on the network with a narrow island platform shared by northbound and southbound trains

clapham-common.history: Opened on 3 June 1900 as the original southern terminus of the City & South London Railway, remaining the end of the line for 26 years until the Morden extension opened in 1926. The station was substantially rebuilt by Charles Holden in 1923–24, gaining a distinctive circular domed entrance building in faience with a glazed dome crowned by a ball finial - one of the most recognisable of Holden's early Underground designs. Like its neighbour Clapham North, it retains a rare narrow island platform in the deep-level tunnel. It also contains a deep-level wartime air raid shelter.
funFacts: The domed entrance pavilion was designed by Charles Holden in 1923 and became one of the most photographed of his early Underground buildings
Was the southernmost station on the Underground for 26 years - longer than any other terminus on the line

clapham-south.history: Opened on 13 September 1926 as part of the Morden extension of the City & South London Railway, designed by Charles Holden in his characteristic Portland stone modernist style with a circular ticket hall beneath a concrete dome. One of eight London Underground stations with a deep-level air raid shelter underneath it - in 1948 that shelter became temporary accommodation for nearly 200 Windrush-generation immigrants from the West Indies, one of the first points of settlement for the HMT Empire Windrush arrivals. The shelter has been open for guided tours through the London Transport Museum's Hidden London programme since 2016.
funFacts: The deep shelter beneath the station housed Windrush-generation immigrants in 1948 while the Colonial Office arranged more permanent accommodation across London
The shelter can be visited today on London Transport Museum Hidden London tours

balham.history: Opened on 13 September 1926 as part of the Morden extension, designed by Charles Holden - his first major project for the Underground. On the night of 14 October 1940, a 1,400kg bomb struck the road above the northern end of the platform tunnels, creating a crater into which an out-of-service bus crashed. The blast caused the northbound tunnel to partially collapse and fractured water mains and sewers flooded the platforms, killing 66 people sheltering inside. The damage closed the line between Tooting Bec and Clapham South until January 1941. The station also interchanges with National Rail services on the Brighton Main Line.
funFacts: The 1940 Blitz disaster at Balham was one of the deadliest single incidents on the Underground during World War Two
An out-of-service bus fell into the bomb crater above the station during the raid, adding to the collapse

tooting-bec.history: Opened on 13 September 1926 as Trinity Road (Tooting Bec) as part of the Morden extension, designed by Charles Holden. Renamed Tooting Bec on 1 October 1950. The station is notable for a narrow satellite building on the east side of the junction that provides pedestrian subway access - unusual in that it displays a large glazed roundel on each of its three screen panels, whereas most Morden extension stations have the roundel only in the central panel.
funFacts: Originally named Trinity Road - it kept the parenthetical 'Tooting Bec' on maps until a full rename in 1950

tooting-broadway.history: Opened on 13 September 1926 as part of the Morden extension, designed by Charles Holden. Has a reversing siding between the two running tunnels south of the station, allowing southbound trains to terminate here and return north without going to Morden. The siding was the scene of two serious incidents: a train struck the end wall in 1960 injuring the driver, and in 1971 a train crashed at around 30mph, killing the driver in an incident similar to the later Moorgate disaster.
funFacts: Southbound trains occasionally terminate here rather than Morden - a reversing siding between the tunnels allows them to switch direction without reaching the terminus

colliers-wood.history: Opened on 13 September 1926 as part of the Morden extension, designed by Charles Holden in his Portland stone modernist style with a three-part glazed screen facade whose column capitals are three-dimensional versions of the Underground roundel. The pub directly across the road from the station is named The Charles Holden in honour of its architect.
funFacts: The pub opposite the station is named The Charles Holden - one of the few pubs in London named after a transport architect

south-wimbledon.history: Opened on 13 September 1926 as part of the Morden extension, originally planned under the name Merton Grove and shown on tube maps as South Wimbledon (Merton) from 1928 until the parenthetical was dropped in the early 1950s. Designed by Charles Holden in his Portland stone modernist style. It is the southernmost Underground station to have its platforms in tunnel - the next station, Morden, is built in an open cutting.
funFacts: Originally planned to be called Merton Grove before the extension was built
The last station on the network where you're underground - Morden, one stop south, is in an open cutting

morden.history: Opened on 13 September 1926 as the southern terminus of the Morden extension and the southernmost station on the entire Underground network, a distinction it still holds today. Built on open farmland - Morden in 1926 was a rural village - Charles Holden had more space here than at any other station on the extension, and designed a broad Portland stone facade with flanking shop parades and provision for an office building above, which was eventually added in the 1960s. From opening day Morden served as the hub for extensive bus routes into suburban south London, with the Southern Railway estimating it lost around four million passengers a year to the new tube. The tunnel between Morden and East Finchley, at 17.25 miles, was the longest railway tunnel in the world when it opened.
funFacts: The southernmost station on the entire London Underground network
The 17.25-mile tunnel between Morden and East Finchley was the longest railway tunnel in the world when it opened in 1926
Morden was a rural village with a population of around 1,300 in 1921 - by 1951 it had grown to over 35,000, driven almost entirely by the arrival of the tube

nine-elms.history: Opened on 20 September 2021 as part of the Northern line extension to Battersea, the first major Underground extension since the Jubilee line reached Stratford in 1999. The station serves the rapidly developing Nine Elms regeneration area, home to New Covent Garden Market and the Embassy of the United States, which relocated from Mayfair in 2017. The extension was funded largely by private developers rather than public funds, with contributions from the Battersea Power Station redevelopment and surrounding Nine Elms schemes totalling around £260 million.
funFacts: The US Embassy relocated to Nine Elms from Grosvenor Square in 2017 - its presence helped justify the case for the new tube station

battersea-power-station.history: Opened on 20 September 2021 as the terminus of the Northern line extension to Battersea, the first new Northern line station in 95 years. The station sits beneath the redeveloped Battersea Power Station, a Grade II* listed coal-fired power station that operated from the 1930s until its closure in 1983. Construction of the extension cost £1.1 billion - £160 million under budget - with tunnelling carried out by two tunnel boring machines named Helen and Amy. The station is the only one on the London Underground network to include the word 'station' in its official name.
funFacts: The only station on the entire Underground network whose name includes the word 'station' - making it officially 'Battersea Power Station station'
Tunnelling was done by two boring machines named Helen and Amy, which departed from the Battersea end in March 2017
No new roundels had been made for a Northern line station since Morden opened in 1926 - until these two stations were built

acton-main-line.history: Opened on 1 February 1868 by the Great Western Railway as simply Acton, serving passengers on the expanding suburban network west of Paddington. Renamed Acton Main Line on 26 September 1949 to distinguish it from the nearby Underground stations at West Acton and North Acton. Together with those two stations it borders the GWR garden estate, a large area of family housing built by GWR in the 1920s to house its staff - particularly drivers from the Old Oak Common depot. The Victorian station building was demolished in 1974 and replaced with a modest booking office; the station was subsequently rebuilt with step-free access as part of the Crossrail project.
funFacts: The station is one of three - with West Acton and North Acton - that form a triangle around the GWR garden estate, built in the 1920s to house railway workers from the Old Oak Common depot

west-ealing.history: Opened on 1 March 1871 as Castle Hill on the Great Western Railway, renamed Castle Hill Ealing Dean in 1875, and finally West Ealing in 1899. From 1883 to 1885 the station was served by District Railway trains running the short-lived Mansion House to Windsor service, one of the earliest attempts to connect the Underground network with the Great Western Main Line. The station became part of the Elizabeth line in 2018 when TfL Rail took over services from Paddington to Hayes & Harlington.

hanwell.history: One of the earliest stations on the Great Western Railway, opening on 1 December 1838 just months after the line first ran between Paddington and Maidenhead. The station was rebuilt around 1875–77, moving 250 metres east of its original location when the main line was quadrupled, and the rebuilt building is now a Grade II listed structure. It was renamed Hanwell and Elthorne in 1896, reverting to plain Hanwell in 1974. The station sits a short distance from the Grade I listed Wharncliffe Viaduct - one of Isambard Kingdom Brunel's finest early engineering works - and some original platform nameboards bearing the old Hanwell and Elthorne name are still visible.
funFacts: Some original platform nameboards still carry the pre-1974 name 'Hanwell and Elthorne' - they were never fully replaced
The nearby Wharncliffe Viaduct, completed in 1837, was the first railway viaduct built in the London area and was designed by Brunel

southall.history: Opened on 1 May 1839 by the Great Western Railway, less than a year after the line first ran from Paddington. Like its neighbours, the station was briefly served by District Railway trains on the Mansion House to Windsor service between 1883 and 1885. Southall is notable for its bilingual platform signage in both English and Punjabi Gurmukhi script, reflecting the area's large Punjabi community - one of the few stations in England to display bilingual signage. The station was rebuilt with step-free access as part of the Crossrail project.
funFacts: One of the few railway stations in England with bilingual signage - platform signs display both English and Punjabi Gurmukhi script

hayes-harlington.history: Opened in 1868 by the Great Western Railway as Hayes, serving the communities of Hayes and Harlington in west London. Like other stations along the line it was briefly served by District Railway Windsor trains between 1883 and 1885. The station is the junction point for the Heathrow Airport spur, with trains branching off here via a dedicated tunnel and flyover junction built for the Heathrow Express in the 1990s. In the early twentieth century the nearby EMI factory - where records by The Beatles, Pink Floyd and countless others were pressed - brought a generation of music industry workers through the station.
funFacts: The EMI pressing factory near the station produced records for The Beatles and Pink Floyd - millions of iconic records passed through Hayes on their way to the world
The Heathrow spur branches from the main line here via a flyover junction, allowing airport-bound and Reading-bound trains to pass simultaneously without conflict

heathrow-terminal-4.history: Opened on 23 June 1998 alongside the Heathrow Express, initially serving as the terminus for all Heathrow Express services from Paddington before Terminal 5 opened in 2008. From 2008 the station was served by a shuttle service from Terminals 2 & 3 rather than direct Paddington trains, and most Heathrow Connect services then terminated at the main terminal. The station briefly closed during the COVID-19 pandemic when Terminal 4 itself was mothballed in 2020, reopening in 2022 as part of the Elizabeth line network.
funFacts: Was the terminus of all Heathrow Express services for its first decade - before Terminal 5 opened in 2008 and took over that role

hanger-lane.history: Opened on 30 June 1947 when the Central line absorbed the former Great Western Railway surface line west of North Acton. The station serves the Hanger Lane gyratory, one of west London's most notorious road junctions, and the surrounding residential areas of Ealing and Alperton.

perivale.history: Opened on 30 June 1947 as part of the Central line's westward expansion onto former Great Western Railway tracks. Perivale is a quiet suburban station serving a largely residential area of the London Borough of Ealing, with the River Brent running nearby.

greenford.history: Opened on 30 June 1947, Greenford was the initial western terminus of the Central line's new branch before the extension to West Ruislip opened the following year. The station is notable for being one of the few Underground stations served by a surface-level GWR shuttle service to Ealing Broadway, which still runs today as part of the Elizabeth line network.

northolt.history: Opened on 21 November 1948 on the final extension of the Central line to West Ruislip. The station serves Northolt, a suburban district in the London Borough of Ealing, and lies close to RAF Northolt, one of the oldest operational military airfields in the world.

south-ruislip.history: Opened on 21 November 1948 as part of the Central line's final westward extension. South Ruislip station sits on the boundary of Ealing and Hillingdon and was formerly served by GWR main line services before the Underground took over the route.

ruislip-gardens.history: Opened on 21 November 1948, Ruislip Gardens is the quietest station on the West Ruislip branch, serving a small residential area between South Ruislip and West Ruislip. The station is one of the least-used on the entire Underground network.

west-ruislip.history: Opened on 21 November 1948 as the western terminus of the Central line. West Ruislip is the end of the line in every sense - a leafy, suburban terminus in the London Borough of Hillingdon where Central line trains have terminated for over 75 years. The station shares its site with a depot and retains a surface-level feel typical of the former GWR branch it replaced.

trafalgar-square.history: Trafalgar Square station opened on 10 March 1906 as part of the Baker Street and Waterloo Railway's first southward extension. The Northern line arrived the following year with its own platforms at the same location, initially called 'Strand' and renamed Charing Cross in 1915. The station took its name from Trafalgar Square above - one of London's most prominent public spaces and a gathering point for national events since its completion in the 1840s. In 1979 the station closed and was immediately replaced by the new Charing Cross station at the same site, renamed to mark the arrival of the Jubilee line.

east-finchley.history: Opened in 1940 when the Northern line took over the former Great Northern Railway branch as part of the Northern Heights electrification scheme. The station was designed by Charles Holden in a striking modernist style, featuring a white rendered facade and a bronze figure known as The Archer on the roof - sculpted by Eric Aumonier, it depicts a bowman shooting an arrow southward toward London. The statue has become one of the most recognisable pieces of public art on the Underground.
funFacts: The Northern Heights scheme, of which East Finchley was a part, was never fully completed - branches planned toward Alexandra Palace and beyond were abandoned after the Second World War, leaving the extension as a permanent fragment of a more ambitious network.

highgate.history: Opened on 22 May 1941 as part of the Northern Heights electrification scheme, which brought former Great Northern Railway lines under London Transport control. The station has two sets of platforms: the active Northern line platforms, and a set of disused tunnels cut for the Alexandra Palace branch - a planned extension abandoned after the Second World War before a single passenger train ran. The abandoned platforms are occasionally opened to the public and remain largely intact.
funFacts: The ghost platforms at Highgate were constructed and left dormant in the early 1940s. Decades later they remain among the best-preserved examples of unfinished Underground infrastructure on the network - a frozen snapshot of a line that never was.

archway.history: Opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway as one of the original stations on what became the Northern line. Named Highgate at opening, it was renamed Archway in 1939 when the Northern Heights station at Highgate opened nearby, to avoid passengers confusing the two.

brent-cross.history: Opened on 19 November 1923 as part of the Northern line's extension to Edgware. A residential station in north-west London, it later became associated with the nearby Brent Cross Shopping Centre, which opened in 1976 as one of the first enclosed shopping centres in Britain.

burnt-oak.history: Opened on 27 October 1924 on the Northern line's Edgware branch. The station was developed to serve the London County Council's Watling Estate, one of the largest interwar council housing estates in London, built to rehouse families from overcrowded inner-city areas.

colindale.history: Opened on 18 August 1924 on the Northern line's Edgware branch. The station sits adjacent to the former RAF Hendon aerodrome site, now home to the RAF Museum, which holds one of the largest collections of historic aircraft in the world.

edgware.history: Opened on 18 August 1924 as the northern terminus of the Hampstead Tube's extension from Golders Green, Edgware was the moment the deep-level tube finally broke out into open country. Until that point the village had been a small market town on the old Roman Watling Street; within a decade of the station opening, intensive Metro-land-style suburban development by builders such as George Cross had transformed the surrounding farmland into the dense residential suburbs of Edgware, Burnt Oak and Canons Park. The station was designed by Stanley Heaps, the Underground Group's chief architect, in a restrained classical idiom with a colonnaded forecourt of paired Doric columns sheltering the ticket hall - a deliberately civic frontage intended to anchor the new town centre - and is Grade II listed for its architectural significance. In the late 1930s London Transport drew up the ambitious Northern Heights plan to extend the line a further five miles north through Brockley Hill and Elstree South to a new terminus at Bushey Heath, with a vast new train depot at Aldenham to serve it. Earthworks and bridge construction began in 1939 but were halted by the Second World War, and the post-war designation of the Metropolitan Green Belt killed the scheme outright. Edgware has been the permanent terminus of the branch ever since, and through services were absorbed into the unified Northern line on its formal naming in 1937.
funFacts: The unfinished Northern Heights extension left a visible trail of physical remains in the countryside north of the station. Embankments, bridge abutments and a partially excavated cutting at Brockley Hill - just over a mile beyond Edgware - still survive in the green belt today, marking the line of the tube tracks that were meant to push on to Elstree and Bushey Heath before the war and the Green Belt designation killed the project for good in 1954.

The vast new train depot planned at Aldenham, on the far side of the never-built Bushey Heath terminus, had been substantially constructed by 1940. With no tube line ever reaching it, the site was repurposed by London Transport as a bus overhaul works - and for more than three decades served as the central maintenance facility where every Routemaster in London service was stripped down and rebuilt on a four-year cycle, before finally closing in 1986.

finchley-central.history: Originally opened as a Great Northern Railway station in 1867 under the name Finchley & Hendon, the station was renamed Finchley (Church End) in 1872. It joined the Northern line on 14 April 1940 as part of the Northern Heights electrification scheme and was renamed Finchley Central at that point, serving the residential suburbs of North Finchley.

hendon-central.history: Opened on 19 November 1923 as part of the Northern line's extension to Edgware, serving the historic market town of Hendon. The station was a catalyst for rapid suburban development in the area during the 1920s and 1930s, when Hendon grew from a small town into a major residential district.

high-barnet.history: The Northern line reached High Barnet in April 1940 when London Transport took over the former Great Northern Railway branch as part of the Northern Heights scheme. As the northern terminus of the Charing Cross branch, it marks the end of the line for trains from Morden and Kennington via the City.

kentish-town.history: Opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway as part of the original Hampstead tube, one of the deepest lines built in London at the time. It serves the residential neighbourhood of Kentish Town between Camden Town and Tufnell Park on the Northern line's Charing Cross branch.

mill-hill-east.history: Opened to Northern line services in 1941 when London Transport took over the former Great Northern Railway branch from Finchley Central. The line was originally intended to continue to Edgware to form a loop with the existing branch, but the extension was abandoned after the Second World War and never built.
funFacts: Mill Hill East exists as a one-station spur only because the Second World War halted its planned continuation. The Northern Heights scheme intended the station as a through stop on a loop connecting the Edgware and High Barnet branches via Mill Hill Broadway, but only the short section from Finchley Central was ever electrified and opened to passengers, on 18 May 1941. The further extension west was abandoned in the post-war retrenchment that also killed the planned Bushey Heath terminus beyond Edgware, and Mill Hill East has been the dead end of an otherwise complete plan ever since.

totteridge-whetstone.history: Opened to Northern line services in April 1940 as part of the Northern Heights scheme, when London Transport assumed control of the former Great Northern Railway branch. The station serves the affluent suburbs of Totteridge and Whetstone on the southern fringe of the London Green Belt.

tufnell-park.history: Opened on 22 June 1907 on the Charing Cross, Euston and Hampstead Railway as part of the original Hampstead tube. A quiet residential station on the Northern line's Charing Cross branch, it serves the neighbourhood of Tufnell Park between Kentish Town and Archway.

west-finchley.history: Opened to Northern line services in 1940 as part of the Northern Heights electrification scheme, taking over from the former Great Northern Railway service. A residential station between Woodside Park and Finchley Central, it serves the western fringe of the Finchley suburbs.
funFacts: West Finchley spent only seven years as a main-line railway station before the Underground took it over. The London and North Eastern Railway, successor to the Great Northern, opened the new halt on 1 March 1933 to serve recent suburban housing between the existing Finchley Central and Woodside Park stations; less than a decade later the whole branch was electrified and absorbed into the Northern Heights extension of the Northern line, making West Finchley one of the shortest-lived main-line halts on any route the Underground inherited intact.

woodside-park.history: Opened to Northern line services in 1940 as part of the Northern Heights electrification scheme. The station serves the residential suburb of Woodside Park in the London Borough of Barnet, on the High Barnet branch of the Northern line.

upney.history: Opened on 30 May 1932 as part of the District line's Upminster extension. The station serves the Upney area of Barking, between Barking and Becontree.

becontree.history: Becontree opened on 18 June 1932 as part of the District line's Upminster extension, built to serve the rapidly growing Becontree Estate. The estate, constructed by the London County Council between 1921 and 1935, was one of the largest public housing developments in the world at the time, eventually accommodating around 100,000 people across more than 25,000 homes. A predecessor steam-train halt at Gale Street had served the new estate from 1926; the District's arrival in 1932 brought electric service and finally connected residents to central London by Underground.
funFacts: The Becontree Estate was conceived under the 1919 'Homes Fit for Heroes' campaign, a Lloyd George initiative aimed at clearing inner-London slums and rehousing First World War veterans.

dagenham-heathway.history: Opened on 12 September 1932 as 'Heathway' on the District line's Upminster extension, and renamed Dagenham Heathway in 1949. The station serves the heart of postwar Dagenham.

dagenham-east.history: Originally opened on 1 May 1885 by the London, Tilbury and Southend Railway as 'Dagenham'. The District line arrived in 1932 and the station was renamed Dagenham East in 1949 to distinguish it from the newer Dagenham Heathway.

elm-park.history: Opened on 13 May 1935 to serve the new interwar Elm Park housing estate. The station was a late infill on the District line's Upminster extension, filling the gap between Dagenham East and Hornchurch.

hornchurch.history: Opened on 1 May 1885 by the London, Tilbury and Southend Railway. The District line first served Hornchurch in 1902, but through trains were withdrawn from 1905 to 1932 due to incompatibility with the LTSR's steam-only line.

upminster-bridge.history: Opened on 17 December 1934 as a late infill station on the District line's Upminster extension, between Hornchurch and Upminster. The station takes its name from the road bridge carrying traffic over the railway beside the crossing of the River Ingrebourne.

upminster.history: Upminster opened on 1 May 1885 as a London, Tilbury and Southend Railway station serving the village on the eastern edge of London. The District line first reached the station in June 1902, but through services were withdrawn in 1905 because the District's electric trains were incompatible with the LTSR's steam-only line. The District returned permanently in 1932 when the route was electrified to Upminster, where it has terminated ever since. The station remains a major interchange: District line platforms share the site with c2c mainline services to Fenchurch Street and the Essex coast, and the London Overground reaches Upminster via the Romford branch.
funFacts: Upminster is the easternmost station on the entire London Underground network.

maryland.history: Opened on 6 January 1873 by the Great Eastern Railway. The station takes its name from Maryland Point, a local area reputedly named by a 17th-century resident returning from the American colony of Maryland.

forest-gate.history: Opened on 20 June 1840 by the Eastern Counties Railway as part of its pioneering London to Romford line, one of the earliest railways in east London. The station serves the residential Forest Gate area between Stratford and Manor Park.

manor-park.history: Opened on 6 August 1872 by the Great Eastern Railway to serve a then-developing suburb between Forest Gate and Ilford.

ilford.history: Opened on 20 June 1839 by the Eastern Counties Railway, making it one of the very earliest railway stations serving the London area. The station has long been the principal interchange for central Ilford and now sees Elizabeth line trains alongside Greater Anglia services.

seven-kings.history: Opened on 1 March 1899 by the Great Eastern Railway to serve the developing residential area between Ilford and Goodmayes.

goodmayes.history: Opened on 8 February 1901 by the Great Eastern Railway to serve the new Goodmayes housing estate then being built east of Seven Kings.

chadwell-heath.history: Opened on 12 December 1864 by the Great Eastern Railway, originally serving a rural Essex village. The surrounding area was suburbanised in the early 20th century and the station now sits within the London Borough of Redbridge.

romford.history: Romford station opened on 20 June 1839 as the eastern terminus of the Eastern Counties Railway's London extension, making it one of the very first railway stations in the London area. The ECR line was rapidly extended east - to Brentwood in 1840 and Colchester in 1843 - and Romford became a busy junction with the opening of the Romford-Upminster branch in 1893. Its role grew steadily through the GER, LNER, and British Rail eras into one of east London's principal commuter interchanges. Elizabeth line services arrived in 2022, restoring rapid central-London access to a market town with railway roots going back nearly two centuries.
funFacts: Romford was home to one of England's oldest livestock markets - founded by royal charter in 1247 and operating continuously until 1958 - and the arrival of the railway in 1839 quickly turned the station into a key conduit for moving cattle from Essex farms to the City of London.

gidea-park.history: Opened on 1 December 1910 by the Great Eastern Railway as 'Squirrels Heath and Gidea Park', and renamed Gidea Park in 1913. The station was built specifically to serve the new Gidea Park garden suburb developed in the years before the First World War.

harold-wood.history: Opened on 1 December 1868 by the Great Eastern Railway. The station serves the residential area of Harold Wood on the eastern edge of Greater London.

brentwood.history: Opened on 1 July 1840 by the Eastern Counties Railway, briefly serving as the line's eastern terminus before the extension toward Colchester. The station sits on the Brentwood escarpment in the Borough of Brentwood, Essex.

shenfield.history: Opened on 29 June 1843 by the Eastern Counties Railway as part of its extension from Brentwood toward Colchester. Shenfield is the eastern terminus of the Elizabeth line, with onward Greater Anglia services to Norwich and other Essex destinations.

snaresbrook.history: Opened on 22 August 1856 by the Eastern Counties Railway as part of its Loughton branch. The station was transferred to the Central line on 14 December 1947 as part of the postwar New Works Programme extension into Essex.

south-woodford.history: Opened on 22 August 1856 by the Eastern Counties Railway as 'George Lane', and renamed South Woodford in 1937. The station was transferred to the Central line on 14 December 1947.

woodford.history: Opened on 22 August 1856 by the Eastern Counties Railway, becoming a junction in 1903 when the Hainault loop opened to its south. The station was transferred to the Central line on 14 December 1947 as part of the New Works Programme.

buckhurst-hill.history: Opened on 22 August 1856 by the Eastern Counties Railway as part of its Loughton branch. The station transferred to the Central line on 21 November 1948 as the eastern extension reached north toward Loughton.

loughton.history: Loughton first opened on 22 August 1856 as the terminus of the Eastern Counties Railway's Woodford branch, slightly south of the current site. The present station building - designed by John Murray Easton for the New Works Programme - was completed in 1940 with curved brick canopies and clean modernist lines, but the planned transfer to the Central line was delayed by the Second World War. Central line services finally reached Loughton on 21 November 1948. The station was Grade II listed in 1995 as one of the finest interwar Underground stations outside central London.
funFacts: The Central line's extension to Loughton was promoted in part as a way to give Londoners easier access to Epping Forest - the 6,000-acre ancient woodland granted to the City of London under the Epping Forest Act 1878, which preserves it as a free public space in perpetuity.

debden.history: Opened on 24 April 1865 by the Great Eastern Railway as 'Chigwell Road', soon renamed 'Chigwell Lane'. The station took its current name Debden when the Central line absorbed the route on 25 September 1949.

theydon-bois.history: Opened on 24 April 1865 by the Great Eastern Railway as part of the Loughton-Ongar extension through Epping Forest. The station transferred to the Central line on 25 September 1949.

epping.history: Opened on 24 April 1865 by the Great Eastern Railway as part of the line's extension toward Ongar. Epping became the eastern terminus of the Central line on 25 September 1949 and has remained so since the Ongar branch beyond closed in 1994.
